items.title: Flare Editor | A flare configurator tool for designing presets for the LensFlare module

posts.author: fawrza
posts.content: Flare Editor 1876×253 69.2 KB Note: If you are looking for the base “LensFlare” module, please refer to this post here. Lens flare system, easily usable by builders or scripters Community Resources Hello all, Today I’m releasing a simple lens flare system I wrote for an upcoming game of mine. This uses ParticleEmitters to achieve a lens flare effect, which also gives nice bloom and also allows for additive blending. This system is meant to be simple to use, so customization of lens flares is mainly achieved by using CollectionService to tag parts to put flares on, as well as attributes to customize flare type, distance, and strength. Video Demonstration [Lens Flares in Roblox!] … Purchase from Creator Store: Greetings. I have been experimenting with @gluGPU ’s lens flare module, and I’ve decided to make it definitive by applying required optimizations and perfecting the occlusion/brightness issues. However, I still ran into an issue. If I had to configure the lens flares, there weren’t much options for non-programmers/non-coders. Additionally, the process of configuration was extremely tedious and time-consuming. I am proposing a solution. Today, I am releasing BOTH the newly modified LensFlare system, as well as the LensFlareEditor plugin. lensFlarePreview.jpg 894×689 28.4 KB The New LensFlare Module (Flare 2): Installation: Get the Module: Grab the updated LensFlare Package from the Creator Store and drag it into ReplicatedStorage . Get the Plugin: Install the Flare Editor from the same package to visually customize your styles. Enable the Init Script: Place the InitLensFlare local script inside StarterPlayer.StarterPlayerScripts (this automatically manages all flares and handles the Sun glare out-of-the-box). Tag Your Lights: Select any Part or Attachment in your Workspace that you want to shine, and add the tag "LensFlare" to it using Roblox’s built-in Tag Editor or our plugin. Installation instructions are included in the package Features Improvements: The new module features QOL and optimizations. QOL: Added adaptive, smoother occlusion for the Lens Flares, as opposed to the intermittent occlusion Fixed the LensFlare confliction/emitter brightness issue Flare Smoothing when out of composition Tweens brightness over transparency Tweens component size during occlusion Supports FlarePart size color (size = scale) Added attributes "LensFlareAlpha" -- Controls the transparency of the flare "LensFlareDistance" -- Controls the distance of the flare from the camera "LensFlareSize" -- Controls the size of the flare "LensFlareScale" -- Controls the scale of the flare "LensFlareStyle" -- Select the LensFlare preset you would like to apply. e.g. "Default", "Anamorphic". Optimizations: Attachment Pooling for the ParticleEmitters holding the flare elements. Single shared BindToRenderStep with an internal renderList, bound only when at least one flare exists. Old bound one render-step callback per flare. Per-flare ring jitter via golden ratio, many flares

posts.author: gluGPU
posts.content: Like fawrza says, occlusion checking runs on the CPU, not as a shader on the GPU, so there is less headroom for performance. Additionally, both systems use ParticleEmitters to draw flares onto the screen, which involves stacking a lot of transparent elements on top of each other, which will naturally lead to a lot of overdraw (especially with more complex presets). I don’t know how complex the fragment shader for particle emitters is but stacking a bunch of transparent elements is basically worst case scenario, so it’s somewhat of a miracle that this runs as well as it does–although I don’t mean to discredit the work that fawrza has done, it’s genuinely incredible. If you’re smart with how you implement the effect, it shouldn’t be too impactful on performance!

items.title: DataStore: UpdateAsync pcall failure does not guarantee write did not commit, no way to distinguish timeout-but-committed from real failure

posts.author: CoderMitchell
posts.content: When DataStore:UpdateAsync completes the backend write (step 4 of its internal sequence) but the HTTP response back to the game server is lost or times out (step 5), pcall returns false. The caller has no way to distinguish this from a genuine write failure where the data was never committed. This produces incorrect game state when the caller uses pcall failure to trigger rollback or refund logic. Compounding this, the natural mitigation — verifying the write with GetAsync before refunding — silently returns stale data because GetAsync’s local cache (4-second TTL) is not invalidated when UpdateAsync times out. The verification passes against pre-write cached data, and the rollback proceeds incorrectly. Steps to reproduce: Create two DataStore keys: “sender” and “recipient.” Write initial data to both keys (e.g., sender has an item, recipient does not). In a Script, remove the item from the sender’s local state. Call DataStore:UpdateAsync on the recipient’s key inside a pcall, adding the item to the recipient’s data via the transform function. Simulate or wait for a network timeout on the response (this occurs naturally under load, throttling, or transient service issues — error codes 501, 502 with HttpError: Timedout). Observe that pcall returns false. Immediately call DataStore:GetAsync on the recipient’s key to check whether the item was delivered. Observe that GetAsync returns the OLD data (without the item) because it hits the local cache, which was not invalidated by the timed-out UpdateAsync. Based on both pcall failure and the GetAsync verification, the rollback logic concludes the write failed and refunds the item to the sender. Call DataStore:GetAsync on the recipient’s key again AFTER the cache TTL expires ( 4 seconds), or use DataStoreGetOptions with UseCache = false. Observe that the recipient’s data DOES contain the item — the write committed despite the timeout. The item now exists in both the sender’s inventory (refunded) and the recipient’s data (committed). Incorrect state. This is not intermittent once the timeout-but-committed condition occurs — the cache behavior guarantees that naive verification fails 100% of the time in this scenario. Affected systems include any game with cross-key rollback logic: trading, gifting/mailbox, auction houses, guild banks, cross-server transfers. Relevant documentation references: Error codes and limits: https://create.roblox.com/docs/cloud-services/data-stores/error-codes-and-limits Caching behavior: https://create.roblox.com/docs/cloud-services/data-stores/versioning-listing-and-caching UseCache property: https://create.roblox.com/docs/cloud-services/data-stores/manage-data-stores DataStoreGetOptions source: creator-docs/content/en-us/reference/engine/classes/DataStoreGetOptions.yaml at main · Roblox/creator-docs · GitHub Expected behavior Two expectations, in order of priority: UpdateAsync should provide a way to distinguish “write definitely did not execute” from “write may have committed b

posts.author: zhongbot
posts.content: Thanks for the report, @CoderMitchell . What you described is valid and highlights an important pitfall when handling write failures and immediate verification reads. We’ll be updating our documentation to clarify this behavior and add guidance, including an example pattern for using an uncached read when verifying state after a write error, which addresses your suggestion #2 . Regarding suggestion #1 , distinguishing between “definitely did not succeed” and “final outcome is uncertain” is not always straightforward in distributed data systems, especially around transient failures and timeouts. We agree this is an area worth improving, and we’re continuing to evaluate whether there are ways to make this easier and safer for developers to handle.

items.title: Satset - Hybrid Networking with Zero-Allocation Serialization for Roblox

posts.author: protheeuz
posts.content: Satset Documentation | Source | Releases A high-performance hybrid networking library for Roblox experiences. Zero-allocation serialization with automated frame-level batching. sat·set /sat-sèt/ adjective (slang) : Indonesian colloquialism for being rapid, efficient, and quick to act. Support Me If you find Satset helpful for your Roblox projects, consider supporting its development: Follow on Roblox | Join my Roblox Community | Sponsor on Github Check my other work: Roblox Slang Satset is a Roblox networking library built for high-throughput workloads without trading away memory stability. It supports packet-based messages through Packets and stateful replication through Channels. Current release: v0.4.2 This first post is kept current. Release posts below remain as version history. Packets support reliable and unreliable delivery, frame batching, typed schemas, server-owned Groups, and server-side rate limiting. Channels use dirty-field bitmasks and periodic keyframes for state replication. Satset does not require an IDL or code-generation step. Schemas are written in pure Luau. local Damage = Satset.definePacket({ name = "Damage", schema = { targetId = Satset.Types.u32, amount = Satset.Types.u16, critical = Satset.Types.bool, }, }) Packets Packets are queued into buffer streams and flushed during PostSimulation . Repeated packet ids can share one run header. Direct same-size reliable batches also use an adaptive delta path: general payloads encode changes against the previous batch text remains raw when the delta form does not help eligible bitpacked payloads can rearrange delta bytes before transport compression Fixed-size schemas omit the per-payload size header. -- Client Damage:fireServer({ targetId = 42, amount = 10, critical = false, }) -- Server Damage:listenServer(function(player, data) print(player.Name, data.amount) end) Server-Owned Groups A Group is an explicit set of recipients controlled by the server. Clients cannot subscribe themselves. local RedTeam = Satset.defineGroup("RedTeam") RedTeam:add(player) Damage:fireGroup(RedTeam, { targetId = 42, amount = 10, critical = false, }) RedTeam:remove(player) RedTeam:destroy() fireGroup serializes the payload once, then copies the encoded bytes into each member’s packet stream. Group membership does not change the meaning of Channels. Channels Channels replicate fixed-size state from the server. The first flush sends a keyframe. Later flushes send only fields marked in the dirty bitmask. Periodic keyframes repair drift caused by dropped unreliable updates. local PlayerState = Satset.defineChannel({ name = "PlayerState", schema = { health = Satset.Types.u8, position = Satset.Types.Vector3, }, unreliable = true, resyncInterval = 5, }) -- Server local entity = PlayerState:create(player.UserId, { health = 100, position = Vector3.zero, }) entity:set("health", 85) -- Client PlayerState:subscribe(function(entityId, state) print(entityId, state.health) end) Channels currently replicate globally.

posts.author: TheYusufGamer
posts.content: LVSandals: Roblox uses LZ4 compression on all network traffic This is incorrect. Roblox uses Zstd compression over the network. Libraries like Blink/NetRay/Zap all use fewer raw bytes per packet in general, which is why it’s lower, and for NetRay specifically, I tried to make it optimised for Zstd compression as well. Zstd is fast and built to handle lots of data. Roblox automatically chooses a compression level for the data you send (or it’s a flat level 1). You can test and check your buffer data compression size via EncodingService , setting a compression. LVSandals: Satset strictly caps payloads to 45 KB chunks . This prevents server stalls and disconnects when processing massive bursts of data. Going higher is unlikley to server stall or disconnections, I think the highest I’ve seen a packet being set was in the MB (16MB to 32MB 2^24 to 2^25 bytes), while yes above 50KB/s can cause issues but that depends on other factors aswell If you could provide the studio place file for the benchmark you ran, that would be helpful, and any schema you used for like Blink/Zap. (I know the files are on GitHub, but a place file is just easier)

posts.author: protheeuz
posts.content: Author of Warp here! Thanks for the schemas. I’ve integrated them into my suite and I’m re-running the validation in Studio now. I’ll update the thread with the corrected data as soon as it’s finished. Appreciate the help!

posts.author: TheYusufGamer
posts.content: Benchmark Results I fixed your benchmark as there were some issues. I also only tested NetRay, Blink and SatSet, as you didn’t provide the place file, and these 2 are good enough for comparison for now against SatSet Summary of changes Added actual bandwidth tracking via Bandwidth = Stats.DataSendKbps . Added normalised bandwidth tracking via NormalizedBandwidth , scaled to the target 60 FPS . Fixed the earlier issue where scaled and unscaled bandwidth samples could be mixed into the same array. Added drain duration tracking per tool/bench with DrainDuration . Replaced the fixed drain threshold with a measured baseline bandwidth using measureBaseline(5) . Improved drain detection so it waits until bandwidth stays near baseline for a sustained period instead of exiting on one temporary dip. Added a 60-second timeout to waitForDrain so the benchmark cannot hang forever. Made percentile calculation safer by asserting non-empty samples and using math.ceil nearest-rank indexing. Changed FPS percentile sorting to ascending order, making [p50, min, p80, p90, p95, max] easier to interpret. Added NormalizedBandwidth and DrainDuration to skipped benchmark entries so the result shape stays consistent. Removed the invalid/degenerate camera CFrame setup from the original script. Stopped clearing PlayerGui / destroying PlayerScripts ; instead, rendering/player overhead should be minimised through Studio settings such as disabling CharacterAutoLoads . Added support for NetRay/Blink-style listeners through event.On(callback) . Updated module paths to the current benchmark layout, such as benches.init , modes.Init , and SatSetSource.Satset . Kept server-side receive counts so packet loss can still be calculated after the run. Percentile order: [p50, min, p80, p90, p95, max] Bandwidth is measured by Stats.DataSendKbps . NormalizedBandwidth estimates bandwidth as if the tool sustained the target 60 FPS send rate. DrainDuration is the time spent waiting for outgoing traffic to return near baseline after the 10-second benchmark window. Vectors Tool Sent Received Loss FPS p50 FPS min FPS p95 Bandwidth p50 Kbps Normalized Bandwidth p50 Kbps Drain Duration Roblox 1,013,000 1,013,000 0.00% 116 69 132 44,449,216.00 32,925,345.19 22.20s Satset 997,000 997,000 0.00% 100 99 101 4,795.47 2,877.28 9.13s NetRay 1,906,000 1,906,000 0.00% 190 188 193 248.03 77.90 9.10s Blink 1,577,000 1,577,000 0.00% 158 155 159 203.65 77.67 9.13s Booleans Tool Sent Received Loss FPS p50 FPS min FPS p95 Bandwidth p50 Kbps Normalized Bandwidth p50 Kbps Drain Duration Roblox 75,000 75,000 0.00% 15 15 16 78,920.28 315,681.13 10.23s Satset 1,001,000 1,001,000 0.00% 100 97 104 733.73 440.65 9.11s NetRay 1,117,000 1,117,000 0.00% 112 110 113 18.99 10.22 5.12s Blink 781,000 781,000 0.00% 78 77 80 32.93 25.53 6.66s Mixed Tool Sent Received Loss FPS p50 FPS min FPS p95 Bandwidth p50 Kbps Normalized Bandwidth p50 Kbps Drain Duration Roblox 1,717,000 1,717,000 0.00% 173 162 178 3,276,837.00 1,156,530.71 8.6

posts.author: protheeuz
posts.content: Sorry for the late reply, just saw this! Thanks for the Zstd correction. I’ve updated the post. On the 45KB cap: we do this to prevent CPU spikes and micro-stutters during massive bursts, ensuring a locked 60 FPS. It makes our bandwidth look higher here because Zstd has to reset per chunk, but it’s a trade-off for frame stability. I’m re-running the tests to validate. Thanks!

posts.author: protheeuz
posts.content: Wow, thank you for taking the time to overhaul the benchmark harness! Tracking Stats.DataSendKbps directly and fixing the drain detection is a massive improvement over the original estimation method. I really appreciate the thoroughness here. I see the JSON results you posted at the bottom, but it looks like you didn’t include the actual modified benchmark scripts/code in your post. Could you share the updated code with me? (Even better if you’d be open to submitting a Pull Request to the GitHub repo!). Once I have your updated harness, I’ll gladly merge those improvements, re-run the full suite, and update the main thread with this new, much more accurate tracking methodology. Thanks again for the collaboration!

posts.author: TheYusufGamer
posts.content: Satset bench.rbxl (107.3 KB) Here is the Roblox Studio file. You should be able to just use the local and server script with minimal changes in your own testing suite. I would have done a PR; however, I noticed you did it via Rojo? I didn’t use Rojo for these simple benchmark changes, so it would be easier for you to just copy it over. LVSandals: On the 45KB cap: we do this to prevent CPU spikes and micro-stutters during massive bursts, ensuring a locked 60 FPS. This, I’m not exactly sure about. I don’t think (not exactly sure) sending a large payload actually affects CPU usage, but it would affect FPS, etc., and degrade performance, but either way, the cap did help for FPS in entities specifically. I would also recommend just removing RED from your benchmark, as it seems to just crash in your testing.

posts.author: protheeuz
posts.content: Thanks for the Studio file! Good call on the 45KB cap. Seeing that FPS jump in the entities test was a huge win. I’m actually curious about your point on the CPU usage though. If it’s not a CPU spike, what do you think is actually causing that FPS drop when sending huge payloads? Maybe just the engine’s internal processing overhead? It’s an interesting technical point to dig into. I’ll also pull RED from the next update to keep the focus on the libraries that don’t crash. I’ll have the thread updated shortly!

posts.author: TheYusufGamer
posts.content: I ran the benchmark again to quickly eyeball CPU usage, specifically for the Entities test. Satset showed lower CPU usage than NetRay/Blink there, which is most likely why it had higher FPS. My guess is that this is due to the 45 KB cap reducing the amount of send work done per frame, although I’m not 100% sure if that’s the only factor or if there are other internal differences contributing. So yeah, CPU timing/usage does seem to be the big factor here. Satset’s capped send size appears to reduce per-frame CPU pressure, which improves FPS, but it also resulted in a longer drain time compared to NetRay/Blink, which makes sense if the work is being spread out instead of flushed as aggressively. Memory usage looked good across the board, excluding Roblox of course, and memory pressure can also affect performance through GC/allocation overhead. My main read is that Satset’s higher FPS comes from sending less data per frame, while still using more total bandwidth overall. This reminds me a bit of old dynamic NetRay, where FPS improved when throttling was enabled. Packet also has throttling, so double-check whether that is uncommented/enabled in your benchmark.

posts.author: Voidage
posts.content: I’m not sure if it’s not included because it might be considered bad practice or something. but do you plan to implement a roblox instance type? I assume it’s because you can’t just turn them into a buffer?

posts.author: TheYusufGamer
posts.content: You can’t write instances into buffers; however, a workaround would be saving the reference to the instance and writing and reading that as a buffer. This has its own issues, so just sending it normally is usually the best way.

posts.author: protheeuz
posts.content: Spot on, Asta. Your analysis on CPU usage vs FPS is exactly what I saw in my own tests. I deliberately chose the 45 KB cap to keep the Heartbeat stable. Letting the engine handle 1MB+ of data in one frame usually causes micro-stutters. I decided that a longer drain time, where data is spread across multiple frames, is better for keeping the FPS locked at 60. Smooth gameplay is more important to me than winning a throughput race in a synthetic test. Thanks for the tip on Packet. I’ll double-check those throttling settings to make sure everything is fair

posts.author: protheeuz
posts.content: Regarding the Instance support, Asta is correct here. You can’t serialize Instances directly into raw bytes, which is why I haven’t included them in the buffer types for Satset. I might consider adding a reference-based system for Instances later on, but for now, I’m focusing on pure POD (Plain Old Data) to keep the performance as high as possible. For most cases where you need to replicate Instances, the default Roblox system is still your best bet

posts.author: u5eful
posts.content: Really cool project! I’ve been working on a network myself and I’ve done similar optimizations as those you mentioned. Though I am curious, could you go into more detail about how you achieve “zero-allocation”? It’s really interesting you mention fast paths because I did something similar by basically in lining/repeating a bunch of code, but you don’t seem to have done any of that. Could you discuss what you’re doing specifically to invoke these fastcall instructions? I also noticed you cache the buffer library functions in variables. In my testing this makes 0 difference, did you find some situations where this matters? I think these optimizations are really cool and I wonder just how far we can go when it comes to performance. I’ll be interested to test out Satset and take a closer look at the source code when I get the chance!

posts.author: protheeuz
posts.content: Great questions. I actually struggled with the same code repetition issue before I landed on this design. Zero-allocation dispatch : The main trick is using pcall(func, arg1, arg2, ...) instead of wrapping things in anonymous closures. In Luau, passing arguments directly to pcall prevents a new function allocation every time a packet is dispatched. Combined with a pre-allocated buffer pool in my Batcher, it keeps the memory usage flat even under heavy load. Unrolling : I do some unrolling for common types like Vector3 and string inside Types.array. For simple packets with 1 to 4 fields, the ‘fixed encoder’ I generate is effectively hand-unrolled code. Hope this clears it up! Definitely check out the source when you have a chance. I’d love to hear your thoughts.

items.title: How to achieve this smooth ball physics?

posts.author: TheESPPlayerSeemoney
posts.content: My game: This game (volleyball legends) I’ve read all the articles I could’ve found on this forum about how to achieve the smooth ball physics (that has visually no delay to the user) and ended up in a conclusion that: When player spikes, show the spiker a client-side ball with force applied first, then when the server applies the actual force, show the spiker the actual server ball. However, the result of this conclusion (first video) doesn’t seem to be “smooth.” Rather, it has an unwanted delay during the switch between client-side ball and server-side ball. How could I fix this? Before this, I’ve also made a system that: When player is close enough to the ball, they get network ownership Players that have no ownership has a client-side ball that simulates the server-side ball But one problem I thought of is that it has major security risk since it gives the network ownership entirely to a player (which may be exploited easily.) This ensures more smoothness, but I thought this would be too dangerous for an actual game. Should I just stay with this system?

posts.author: ivan866_z
posts.content: your network ownership approach is somewhat obtuse only the UI for pushing a ball must exist on the client side then use a RemoteEvent and transfer all actual impulse to the server for smooth movement, use either roblox built-in physics decrease the ball mass and friction parameters write your own ball mechanics equations if you want a deformable mesh (liquid body simulation) for the ball, this is a completely different story

posts.author: L0chlainn
posts.content: For a game like this, I don’t imagine the ball is actually physically simulated with forces, but rather it’s done with a kinematic body, directly setting the CFrame of an anchored part, as it can be far easier to create deterministic physics with fewer variables this way. We only need to handle 3 objects in this model, a server ball, a client ball and a visual ball. The server ball is the authority, it represents the real ball, while the client ball is a mimic that just represents the predicted motion of where we think the real ball is. The visual ball will just represent a ball to the player, it’s just there to help us smooth out any stutters in our physics. The server constantly moves the server ball forward, updating its properties (position, velocity and acceleration) it will also send out this information to the clients along with the current server time, so they can keep the client ball updated. The client will take the properties the server gives it (position, velocity and acceleration) and step forward our client ball by the delta time (subtract the current server time from what the server gave us). This is so we compensate for lag, syncing up with the true position of the server ball instead of the the position it was at when it sent the update. We’ll need to wait for the next server update before we can update our client ball again, but we don’t want to have a frozen client ball , so until we get the next update from the server we’ll just predict the position of the server ball locally by stepping forward our client ball every frame. Once we get the next update, we just repeat this process again. If this is done correctly, the client ball and server ball should be in sync and any deviations will be corrected on the next update. The only problem is that when the deviations are corrected, the client ball will visually teleport which can look bad for the player observing it. To fix this, we use the visual ball , which will simply interpolate towards the client ball constantly, so if there’s any teleportation in the client ball , the visual ball will mask it. You can just use AlignPosition inside the visual ball and attach it to the client ball to achieve this easily and you can play around with the forces or just use AlignPosition.RigidityEnabled = true . If you combine this all together, you should have an exact copy if not a better system than what they’re doing in that game.

posts.author: ivan866_z
posts.content: having a moving object on the server side ALREADY utilizes the best known practices of motion interpolation and attempts to make the motion visible on the clients as smooth as possible with current network conditions why use three balls instead of just one?

posts.author: L0chlainn
posts.content: The interpolation you’re talking about only works for objects simulated by Roblox’s dynamic physics system and I believe some of their kinematic constraints like the transform property in Motor6Ds and Animation Constraints. It does not attempt to predict dynamic interactions or implement rollback (Unless you’re using Server Authority, but that would be a bit overkill for just a ball). As such, while yes it uses the “best known practices” it does so for an entirely different client-server model. If your game is lagging for example, parts and players controlled by anyone but the client will freeze until you get the new state from the server, when you do get that state it’ll rubber band to the new state to catch up, but it still only ever works with the data provided instead of predicting future movement and rolling back mispredictions. Some of this is hidden by Roblox when the NetworkOwner mode is set to Auto, since objects around a player might be owned by them so collisions will appear smooth. If it’s owned by another player or the server however, this won’t be the case. You can see it for yourself if you drop a Part in a place file and run part:SetNetworkOwner(nil) and then hit play with some simulated network delay. The part will interpolate yes, but not predict collisions, so when you run into the part it’ll stutter and you’ll be unable to move it until the server simulates it on its side and sends you the result. That system works fine for most games on Roblox and minimises overhead, but it fails when we have competitive games, especially sports. What we need to do instead is create an isolated server authoritative system in our game, which is what I proposed above. The three balls is just conceptual to easily separate our concerns for these three different roles. The reality is the server has authority and client just simulates the ball while respecting that authority. You can implement the server and client entirely in code, only the visual has to be a real part in our world. The server will send the current real state for the client to work with and will validate anything the client says like “I hit the ball”, while also most likely compensating for client lag so that we validate if the hit occurred when the client sent it, rather than when we received it. The client always works with its own ball, but it uses the server as it’s authority and baseline for predictions. By the time the client will receive the servers state, it’s already old data and so we predict where it is in real time straight away using the data it gives us. There’s never a point where we actually use real server state.

posts.author: ivan866_z
posts.content: you are describing your approach in a defensive manner while not explaining why the client in your sports game should not be able to see the actual location of a game critical object (a ball), feeding him a fake although smoothly animated object which will be invalidated on the next hop to the server authority, anyways

posts.author: L0chlainn
posts.content: ivan866_z: while not explaining why the client in your sports game should not be able to see the actual location of a game critical object (a ball) I think you might be struggling to understand what deterministic means. By creating a simple physical simulation using only the kinematic properties to simulate the movement of an object we will always get the same result using the same parameters. By stepping our simulation forward to compensate for latency, we’re using the the real state of the ball as it currently is on the server. The only thing that will effect this is when dynamic interactions occur that we didn’t predict, which is what the rollback is intended to solve for and our interpolation of the visual component smooths out any teleporting effects that could occur as a result. The fake position you’re talking about only occurs when we mis predict the physics, so long as we don’t have any differences in our geometry or latency spikes, this will never really happen. The only exception is when a dynamic interaction occurs, such as when the other player interacts with the ball, which is the same on both models btw, as neither can predict an unpredictable event. What you propose on the other hand will constantly feed the player old data. For example if the latency is 0.5 seconds, then we will only ever see where the ball was 0.5 seconds ago, which while yes it is smoothly interpolated, it’s not the real state of the ball. Neither system will ever guarantee the real state of the world, but while mine predicts the future with a high degree of accuracy due to the minimal dynamic systems involved, your proposal only ever ensures old data that’s already inaccurate by the time it reaches the client.

posts.author: ivan866_z
posts.content: just admit already that you either didn’t know or forgot about the fact there is in fact motion interpolation (most likely the RakNet-based net stack) in Roblox’s server-client replication model what you are proposing is the right way for objects which are not subject for engine built-in mechanisms (such as hitscan bullets in shooter games) implementing your own motion smoothing logic within an already existing one is a mess having a separate ball on each client is a mess, and will fail once the count of players reaches 3 (for it will yield an unsolvable state of a 3-body problem, causing stutter which will be resolved by the collision solver anyways, for it accounts for such cases) normally you should never need rollbacks for visible objects i wonder how would you implement an RTS game with your approach with hundreds of units being controlled by 16 different clients without a fully deterministic server-client model

posts.author: ModzZealous
posts.content: Hey! Would it not work if you have one visual ball on the client and no “real” ball on the server? Instead you create trajectories on the server along with creating a timestamp using workspace:GetServerTimeNow() and then pass the trajectory information along with the timestamp to the client. That way you can simulate the same trajectory and move the ball along by updating the ball’s CFrame to match the trajectory. Also use the timestamp to sync the server and client together so it’s as accurate as possible. Using time to determine the position allows you get it very close to the server’s position by predicting it essentially. Sincerely, Modz

posts.author: L0chlainn
posts.content: You’re clearly just trying to have an argument over a topic you don’t even understand. bro: I’ve read all the articles I could’ve found on this forum about how to achieve the smooth ball physics (that has visually no delay to the user) and ended up in a conclusion that: The OP is clearly asking for a method to hide the visual delay from the players, he’s tried having it be server driven, but the input from client to server causes to much delay that even when he temporarily masks it with a visual, there’s still the matter of the delay. ivan866_z: your network ownership approach is somewhat obtuse only the UI for pushing a ball must exist on the client side then use a RemoteEvent and transfer all actual impulse to the server This does not resolve the issue of delay. There will still be a client to server delay with a remote event and the rendered ball will still be the old state of the server. You haven’t actually resolved anything for them, you’re just spouting nonsense. ivan866_z: implementing your own motion smoothing logic within an already existing one is a mess having a separate ball on each client is a mess, and will fail once the count of players reaches 3 (for it will yield an unsolvable state of a 3-body problem, causing stutter which will be resolved by the collision solver anyways, for it accounts for such cases) normally you should never need rollbacks for visible objects i wonder how would you implement an RTS game with your approach with hundreds of units being controlled by 16 different clients without a fully deterministic server-client model Not a single one of these is an actual issue, you just don’t understand how any of them function. We won’t be implementing it within an existing one, we’re creating our own and bypassing the existing system by not allowing Roblox’s physics to replicate it. We’re using spatial queries and deterministic kinematic systems to resolve our own physical state. The 3 body problem is an issue in dynamic physics when solving for 3 bodies, we’re not doing any of that. We’re solving for 1 body, the ball, which will have a moment in time before the physics is calculated to allow impulses to be applied to the ball. The idea that you shouldn’t apply rollbacks to visible objects is first, untrue and you just made it up. Secondly, I never said to implement it on a visual object. I very clearly stated multiple times that the existence of the visual ball is to mask this rollback, which you still can’t grasp because you don’t understand the topic being discussed. I would never need to apply this to an RTS game, because the delay in that situation is minimal, but if I did, I would apply it under the exact same frame work because, and this may be difficult for you to understand, the math is the same. Instead of just replying with more made up complaints about non-existent issues because you don’t understand how any of this works, make your next reply an explanation as to how your method fixes the unwanted delay the

posts.author: ivan866_z
posts.content: instead of arguing with someone else, why not just realise the built-in algo already does what you are trying to implement if the ball position is outdated by 0.5 seconds - it will display it on each client with an extrapolated position happening 0.5 seconds after the server time if the network congestion makes it impossible updating information for client often enough - the client will display the smoothed interpolated position in between frames you don’t have to transfer an information block form the client which describes how and when the client has hit a fake ball which doesn’t exist anywhere except that particular client - all you have to do is register the keypress (or a jump towards the ball, or whatever) and tell the server this client has attempted hitting the ball; then the server, possessing most accurate information about the ball position, and the positions of all the clients, decides whether the hit happened and how yes, this is called authoritarian server yes, this utilizes motion prediction but you are completely out of touch with the actual usage of the tech you are trying to discuss i repeat again, this stands true if you are using the engine’s physics simulation if your object is being controlled by a completely different system, and you are implementing your own motion laws for the object (such as hitscan bullets) - then yes, your approach is correct but making a game with a ball moving in bizarre ways (such as ball not being affected by gravity, etc.) with an engine using standard physics laws is somewhat strange; transferring network ownership of a game-critical object to client is even worse, for it is an open invitation for exploits

posts.author: ivan866_z

posts.author: L0chlainn

You actually misunderstood this concept of delay previously as well in both my post and the original OP, which I’ve pointed out previously.

posts.author: ivan866_z
posts.content: since you are about to implement your own data exchange, i have noticed you haven’t even mentioned the topology of the network you will be using (star / ring / mesh) moreover, you are not specifying the scalability of your approach as i mentioned, there is high probability it fails once you reach 3 clients, or more and once again, good luck with countering invalid and fraudulent data being sent by clients also notice, implementing custom ‘kinetic approach’ in Roblox will be very problematic, if possible at all - for anything except balls (spheres) or cubes (boxes) - for you will not be able to process collisions with other surfaces because you literally cannot get access to the Part’s vertices (your collision fidelity will be restricted to ‘box’ mode, namely) add the rollback functionality you have proposed - and you get a perfect storm of ambivalence, non-strict physics in your game and complete mess with constantly shifting ownership (including double ownership at any time) P.S. what you call ‘technical jargon’, others call ‘systemic thinking’ what you call ‘multiple editing’, i call ‘verifying information’

posts.author: kaiserandaxl
posts.content: Hello! I would just to have confirmation on your main question and I might be able to provide helpful insight into your problem if you’re still looking for it. Main question: How can you make the Client side of the ball match as closely to the Server Side ball and make the movement match among all the Clients.

posts.author: TheESPPlayerSeemoney
posts.content: Sorry for the late response! I never thought this post would get such recognition! At last, I solved the visual delay problem with my own method. The method basically consists of: 2 balls: Client ball, Server ball Basic Structure Server sends client the physical properties (CFrame, Linear Velocity, Angular Velocity) of the server ball to the client. (Every 1/20 seconds) Client applies the data to the client ball. Visual system Client spikes. → If the client ball is in the spiking range, apply the spiking force to the client ball. → Disable the application of server data to client-side ball. → Send the input to the server via RemoteEvent. Server computes the spike. → If the spike is legitimate, (that spiker’s character is inside the spiking range, …) apply server-side spiking force. + Set the CFrame of the server-side ball to the CFrame of the client-side ball (get it via RemoteFunction, do not adjust CFrame if the distance between server-side ball and client-side ball is too long.) Server realigns the client. → After the server-side force application, fire a RemoteEvent to the spiker from server ( to reconnect the application of server data to client-side ball. Was disabled in (1) ) → When the spiker client receives the event, re-establish the application process and return back to the regular state. Also I’d recommend adding a feature in client-side which forcefully re-establishes the application process if the distance between client-side ball and server-side ball is too much (5 studs recommended.) I hope this helps future scripters and developers. Thank you for reading! Method result

items.title: Developers are not equipped to deal with exploiters

posts.author: LMH_Hutch
posts.content: This is not a post about anti-cheats or the technical aspects of exploiting and exploit prevention. This is a post targeting the severe lack of tooling and support provided by Roblox in regards to exploiting and dealing with generally abusive users. I would encourage readers to share their own testimonials and experiences with exploiters. The intent of this post is to vent frustrations and bring awareness to an ever worsening issue, not to serve as a hub for advice on dealing with these issues. Please considering making proper tutorial posts in # resources:community-tutorials instead. EDIT: This was moved to the feature requests section and some people may not be able to reply. Sorry! My background I have been playing and making games on the platform for almost 10 years. I have developed and assisted in developing multiple large games as well as a number of smaller projects. Over the years I have connected with developers in every field and I’ve been lucky enough to participate in the Accelerator program twice. I have invested a lot into this platform both personally and professionally, both a developer and as a user. I am by no means an authority on exploiting and moderation but I am somebody with years of hands on experience tackling these issues as a developer. My current project (at the time of writing this) is a 100 Robux paid access game with a relatively small average player count (~200). It’s a zombie survival game with some perma-death mechanics. Our ban statistics for the last ~1 year are as follows: September 2021: 390 bans by moderators, 252 bans by anti-cheat October 2021: 427 bans by moderators, 246 bans by anti-cheat November 2021: 409 bans by moderators, 297 bans by anti-cheat December 2021: 433 bans by moderators, 404 bans by anti-cheat January 2022: 370 bans by moderators, 531 bans by anti-cheat February 2022: 403 bans by moderators, 424 bans by the anti-cheat March 2022: 403 bans by moderators, 247 bans by the anti-cheat April 2022: 516 bans by moderators, 386 bans by the anti-cheat May 2022: 756 bans by moderators, 399 bans by the anti-cheat June 2022: 652 bans by moderators, 489 bans by the anti-cheat EDIT: Our game went free to play! I’ve made a follow up reply that contains our opening month ban statistics . I won’t be updating this list anymore or further replying with new ban statistics. These are the accounts that have been caught with sufficient evidence to warrant a ban. Hundreds of names get added to our watch list that have yet to be observed exploiting by our moderation staff. These ban statistics are alarming for me. This is a game with a barrier to entry (100 Robux), and it’s a game with a fairly low population. In our case, user reports of “exploiters in every server” are not at all unfounded. Since implementing a support ticket system in our Discord community we have opened 15638 support tickets where users report exploiters, and 4845 support tickets where users wish to have their account(s) unbanned. These are

posts.author: Polyhex
posts.content: Thanks for sharing. As someone who’s also dealt with similar issues, I sincerely hope Roblox plans to combat exploiting in some way in the future. As it stands now, all we can do is ban exploiters or create our own tools to ban them from our games automatically, which can take up considerable development time and often doesn’t even solve the root problem. If an exploiter is caught, they can just spend 30 seconds creating a new Roblox account and find ways to circumvent bans.

posts.author: AbstractAlex
posts.content: The unfortunate reality is most devs (understandably) end up choosing to prioritize content, whereas games that focus on fighting exploiters get stuck in a neverending battle. Exploits are one of the top things we think about when designing our features experiences, since anything competitive will likely not work well once the exploits are rolled out. I wish Roblox would take legal action against exploit creators distributors, the same way other large companies do.

posts.author: TomskiKiller
posts.content: it’s an issue that can only be fixed by those least likely to listen. I also find it funny how everyone’s first solution is to say “LolManEpic999 used an exploit on my game, so roblox should sue the creator of the exploit!!!”. If Roblox thought it’d be worth their time, they would’ve already done it. The legal team of Roblox isn’t ignorantly unaware of exploits, they just don’t see it worth it. Any damage caused by exploiting would be lesser than what it would cost to sue a big exploit(like Synapse X). The best thing Roblox could do is try to detect injections instead of changing a few numbers every week. It’d also helped if they actually banned people for cheating, instead of kicking for “Unexpected client behavior”, and not allowing the person to play Roblox for 30 minutes.

posts.author: Kampfkarren
posts.content: In Zombie Strike, we automatically poison an account when they use a several year old cheat. We have had 2,311 of these incidents in over 2 years. God only knows how many cheaters we’ve had over the years that were using a recent cheat. Roblox not having any sort of in-game banning system is horrific. A basic HWID/IP ban would do absolute wonders. Every time this is brought up, people mention HWID randomizers (which could be done by exploits), and VPNs. They fail to consider that most people who use exploits are little baby kids who don’t have VPNs. A basic banning system would do miles . It is absolutely miserable that it is an extremely common occurence for someone to literally download cheats mid-game on Roblox, maybe get banned by an admin, then join back on a new account a moment later. This is not even to mention the amount of cheating that is just a part of the Roblox platform. Exploiters can still delete any non-part object in their characters. There is no excuse for this behavior to not be togglable (and then disabled by default). Exploiters can still delete any object in their Player instance if their character is not set. There is no excuse for this behavior to not be togglable (and then disabled by default). (Don’t tell me to track it with scripts, I shouldn’t freakin’ have to!) Automatic animation replication, automatic Motor6D replication, etc are all features that can be nice to have, but should not be baked in to using characters. The frequency of cheaters is an extremely large part as to why I do not play Roblox games anymore. A few months back I joined a game, beat a few people in combat, and got to watch them literally download cheats right then and there. I haven’t gone back to that place since, and it’s not the developer’s fault.

posts.author: Kampfkarren
posts.content: Kampfkarren: HWID randomizers (which could be done by exploits), I explicitly mention this in the post. It’s not a reason to give up and try to do nothing. That’s why it would be nice for IP bans as well. In fact, being able to tell from the developer’s side when someone has a weak HWID randomizer (one that randomizes often) would be an extremely good red flag for developers to look closer into. In a non-Roblox game I manage with thousands of monthly active users, we do both HWID and IP bans. HWID randomizers are common, and yet HWID bans still stop an extreme amount of people, and in other cases, will give a huge red flag for other accounts. For instance: if a person joins on a new account with a banned HWID, and randomizes their HWID, we can poison the account and require they get a new one, adding more barriers to entry. These measures work , I literally use them every day outside of Roblox.

posts.author: TomskiKiller
posts.content: i think the reason people are just so opposed to it is because of fear of developers with bad intentions. plus it could have complications with COPPA as it would allow any game developer, big or small, to collect HWID and IPs from children. even if roblox somehow allows you to ban certain HWIDs and IPs without seeing them, it would never stop the unrational paranoia of users(did you see the unncessary outrage that came from giving users the optional choice to ID verify?). roblox is just really different from any other platform

posts.author: Kampfkarren
posts.content: You don’t need the ability to collect HWIDs to support HWID bans, even though it would catch more of the cases of what I’m talking about. TomskiKiller: even if roblox somehow allows you to ban certain HWIDs and IPs without seeing them, it would never stop the unrational paranoia of users Most users will have no idea it’s even a feature, most users are young children who are playing games with their friends. I don’t care about YouTubers fear mongering or similar, the gains from being able to ban most people overwhelmingly outweighs that.

posts.author: Narroby
posts.content: I have been dealing with banning and general moderation of users in our game for 8 years now. It is absolutely bewildering that a platform like ROBLOX does not offer a proper moderation system. For company that prides itself in providing as many tools as possible to developers, it’s missing this feature that should’ve been present by day one.

posts.author: DEVLocalPlayer
posts.content: The amount of time I put into my game to make sure it has proper measures to combat (most) exploits used by exploiters is horrendous. Not like it really matters since exploiters usually find a way around everything. I was thinking of implementing a client-sided anti-cheat to stop some basic exploits but realized it’s substantially useless considering exploiters can do anything they want with their Roblox client.

posts.author: x_o
posts.content: Adding some more statistics here, my experience is a semi-competitive shooter with around 1.5mil monthly active users: Over the past 7 months our automatic anti-cheat measures have logged over 240,000 instances of accounts exploiting and banned over 150,000 accounts , with our user moderators catching and banning thousands more. Despite our best efforts, exploiters are still a major issue. Players constantly complain about hackers, tell us to ‘get better anticheat’, and have their experiences ruined by kids who downloaded scripts off youtube because they’re that easy to get. A large amount of dev time has to be put toward finding, testing, and patching exploits that could be better spent elsewhere if we were given proper tools to deal with the exploiters we already catch.

posts.author: Quenty
posts.content: Over the last 5 years our mod team has banned over 5000 people from WFYB and associated games. This is through purely user-reported exploits, and represents a very small amount of overall exploits on Roblox. It’s common knowledge that exploits are easily and freely available for any game. It is very common for top developers to be forced to implement the following: A custom ban system and website with ticket handling Automated anti-exploit since not every input or process can be legitimately secured through normal means on Roblox. Manage a moderation team from the game’s community Exploits in certain experiences can be ok (social games, et cetera), but when they ruin the gameplay for others, this becomes unacceptable. It’d be really cool if Roblox could solve this for us, this is literally a non-trivial amount of time for each and every developer right now.

posts.author: grilme99
posts.content: Adding onto statistics here, BedWars has implemented several detections for specific exploits and cheats in the past. Historically, our detections have accumulated tens of thousands of individual accounts in just 72 hours, depending on the time of year. In the past, we’ve been able to have real-time analytics on the number of exploiters actively playing the game and the rate at which legitimate players will play against a cheater. Without going into specifics, those numbers were (and presumably still are) horrendously bad. Roblox needs to provide better tooling for dealing with bad actors at scale. Not only implementing a robust banning mechanism but also tools for moderating games and dealing with reports. Roblox already has a built-in report category for exploits, but it is not exposed to developers.

posts.author: sykoqic
posts.content: Having to extensively sink my own development time and energy into designing systems to deal with exploiters and into designing tools for moderators to deal with exploiters is incredibly exhausting work, and the idea of doing such a thing has completely shut down any motivation I had to work on specific types of projects. When it comes to all this, Roblox is either downright negligent or even working against efforts that developers could employ to combat developers (by patching out weird quirks of the engine that were used to detect exploits on the client-side). The attitude (or rather lack there of) they have taken towards mitigating exploits is quite simply disheartening. Alongside all that, Humanoids and NetworkOwnership as a whole are horribly flawed against exploiters. There is no easy way to prevent most movement / physics exploits in one fell swoop unless you were to go out of your way to develop an server-authoritive networking model for your character (which no developer should ever have to do for all the pain that comes with it) and extensively control and monitor the physics and network owners of in-game physics objects. To emphasize how bad all that is, it is possible to outright kill people with only a tool, and fling them around by just making your character spin erratically. For reference, one of the games I work on has around 30,000 bans, and those are just the ones handed out by moderators.

posts.author: Kampfkarren
posts.content: You misread my post. Literally the entire post before it is arguing in favor of HWID bans.

posts.author: wallofgarlic
posts.content: My bad, I was reading like 3 different post and misread yours.

posts.author: Arch_Mage
posts.content: In Deepwoken, our most recent paid access game, we’ve detected around 60,000 accounts exploiting, and we have around 40,000 bans on record for exploiting in the first 4 months alone. That’s about 8% of the accounts who have purchased the game. In Rogue Lineage, our previous paid access game, we have around 150,000 anti-exploit bans on record, not counting moderator bans. This accounts for about 15% of the accounts who have purchased the game. Players who get banned just make new accounts, buy the game again, and exploit all over again. Their Roblox account suffers no repercussions for this behaviour no matter how many games they exploit. The only exploiters who actually see punishment are the tiny few actually making exploits, not the thousands of script kiddies who buy the exploits. Roblox offers no built-in moderation tooling or support. We have to rely on our in-house moderation and simply hope that our players’ days aren’t ruined too often. Any game with competitive aspects is completely compromised in its integrity by the state of Roblox security. When we think of a fun feature it’s immediately ruined by the realisation that it could just be gamed and trivialised by anyone who has the $20 to buy an injector. Players are just expected to grin and bear it. We have hundreds of support tickets made every single day by players who are the victims of exploiters.

posts.author: wravager
posts.content: I made a Squid Game in September because I wanted to get in on the hype. It got to 900 concurrent players and then multiple hackers went in and flew around the map flinging everyone to their death. The player count dropped to almost 400 a few hours after reports of this activity started. With 100 player servers, all it takes is 1 person to kill your game.

posts.author: Megalodox_Official
posts.content: Citizens are not ready to deal with war, but when it comes we face it. My game (WiP) is insanely difficult to exploit, because we don’t use PlayerModels and it’s a territorial based game instead of a player PvP game, so even by doing ;kill all wouldn’t be able to do any harm. Sadly once it’s popular I’m sure some people will start creating a brand new script just for my game, what should I do? For ranked mode should the account age be over 15 days, pays 1 robux or/and have premium?

posts.author: Ie_fishe
posts.content: In MetaMethod games we have a similar horrible instance, users who create or have stashes upon stashes of alts, generators, friends etc is concerning, none the less that Roblox is such an easily exploitable platform where getting an executor let’s you do wonders. Here’s some of the following that makes absolutely no sense why it’s even possible for the client: Save instance Access core UI but we as developers can’t?? Manipulate with physics objects without server validation allowing for common things like flight Destruction and disabling of scripts mid run session out of studio Ability to switch humanoid states to allow noclip The list goes on and on, it’s understandable Roblox as a platform restricts us from clients and consumers IPs and MAC addresses but as a publicly shared company now with Investors it should be a much higher priority to allow creators which are your lead exposure to the world to actually combat there script kiddies. Steam has no problem collecting your data or VAC banning you as a whole, and it has children games on there as well. So why do us developers have to work around the systems in hope that the anti exploit can actually catch the exploiter and not someone with horrible internet? Not to even mention how easily it’s for game specific exploits to be created, distributed and then cyphered so the developer barely can debug it and find the way to fix it. If you are wanting to appeal to the users on the platform a start would be to stop with moderators, they are a thing of the past no one here will say that they do the best job at reading reports nor actually dealing with those who break ToS. We need systems in place people. Systems that won’t allow simple injector to just execute code easily on client and even if it’s possible then to put in counter measures that would restrict or make it way harder for any exploiter to read your code, steal your hard work or plague the game with their alts.

items.title: Real world building and scripting optimization for Roblox

posts.author: MrChickenRocket
posts.content: This is a guest article from a community expert. We hope you enjoy it! Introduction image 1575×945 210 KB Hi, I’m Peter “ @MrChickenRocket ” McNeill. I’ve been a game developer for over 25 years now, and I have spent a lot of time building games, engines and renderers. I worked on Fruit Ninja, Jetpack Joyride, Candy Crush and a bunch of other mobile phone games and their engines, so mobile performance and optimisation is something I’ve gotten pretty good at. With Roblox, I’ve released a handful of my own games and projects, and from time to time I consult with some very talented games teams to help make their games run better. Usually this involves doing performance profiling, assessing their game world and assets, and making suggestions on how they can do the same things they’re already doing, but in more efficient ways. What I did for this article is build a Roblox game using good building practices, and then optimized the heck out of it for great performance on all Roblox platforms, but especially mobile. I’ll start off by showing you how to health check your own projects, explain some of the theory behind it, and then we’ll go on a tour of some real-world trickery and techniques you can use to make your own projects run faster. The example game is called Cardboard Box Simulator, and it should look somewhat familiar to you if you’ve ever played BIG Games’ “pet simulator” series of games. (Shoutout to Preston for being a good sport about this!) Cardboard Box Simulator is a demo of a simulator game, with the now-standard squiggly map layout, lots of pets, lots of coins, and plenty of new art and themes per area. Project Highlights Rendering and CPU usage are optimized so that it runs on most phones at graphics quality 10 at 60 FPS (frames per second) Dense, interesting looking map, with lots of details and fairly high quality assets ShadowMap lighting technology, so there are nice shadows and lighting 30 pets per player, with advanced techniques to minimize their bandwidth and cpu usage Up to 200 coins per player, with local client physics for the rendering and a spatial grid for low cpu usage on the pickup logic Hides zones you can’t see Hides small details in the distance Fun effects like a fake underwater zone Built and scripted in a very straightforward way that should be easy to follow Uncopylocked and available here: Cardboard Box Simulator - Roblox 988×609 1020 KB Pets, Coins, and Cardboard Boxes - all of the essential elements of any Roblox experience! 988×536 964 KB The average view is around 100 scene drawcalls and about 150k triangles. 989×734 1.14 MB The map is laid out in a squiggly snake pattern with five zones per row and big dividing green walls. This article includes these main chapters: Budgets - How to budget your project Map Building Style - How to build in an optimal way for rendering Precalculated Visibility - Remove stuff behind walls Distance Culling - How to do basic distance culling Spatial Hashing - How to do fast coin

posts.author: MrChickenRocket
posts.content: Appendixes Appendix A - Links to Interesting Things The Game Cardboard Box Simulator - Roblox :PivotTo() Docs PVInstance | Documentation - Roblox Creator Hub :BulkMoveTo() Docs WorldRoot | Documentation - Roblox Creator Hub :GetPartBoundsInRadius() WorldRoot | Documentation - Roblox Creator Hub CollectionService Docs CollectionService | Documentation - Roblox Creator Hub My Social Media x.com Appendix B - Use of Streaming Enabled You should strongly consider using streaming enabled for your own builds: It can reduce your load times and memory usage, sometimes considerably. It is pretty much essential if you want to have a large map and world. It does a great job of limiting your world to a bubble around the player. It’s being improved all the time. Having said that, Cardboard Box Simulator doesn’t use streaming enabled, just because this particular map is cramped and has those big dividing walls, and is a perfect situation to manage visibility manually. Roblox is very well-behaved if you mix techniques like distance culling with streaming enabled, so you should feel free to experiment there. Appendix C - Drawcalls - What are they and why they matter You need to manage your drawcalls carefully in Roblox. Drawcalls are expensive, and even modern phones are still pretty bad at the speed and number of raw drawcalls they can process per frame. As mentioned - drawcalls are generated as part of the scene rendering process. Every time the engine has to stop drawing one type of thing and start drawing another, it generates a new drawcall with an associated time cost (e.g. if you have a stone teapot next to a wood ball, that will take two drawcalls). Where it gets complicated is that the Roblox engine is really good at optimizing drawcalls, and knowing what it can and can’t do can make a huge difference to your scenes performance, especially on mobile. An easy example of a win - as harped on in this article - most of the time, opaque meshes with the same meshId and material will be batched together into a single drawcall due to “geometry instancing,” even if they have different scale, rotation, position or color differences, and is why you can have so many parts. A counterexample - all particle emitters take one drawcall each - making them surprisingly expensive in terms of scene performance. If you have 40 particle emitters on screen, that’s 40 drawcalls, regardless of if they emit a lot of particles or not. Certain things tend to add to your drawcall usage more than you might think: Transparent objects via Part.Transparency can cause unexpected extra drawcalls. Unique TextureIds/DecalIds on texture and decal instances count as their own materials, and will create their own drawcalls. SurfaceGuis and BillboardGuis can chew up drawcalls very quickly. (Use their built-in distance culling!) Beams and particle effects all cost 1 drawcall each. Very complex shadow casting and light usage in Future Lighting can chew up drawcalls in both (Scene) and (Shadow) cat

items.title: Neural Network Library (Obsolete)

posts.author: Kironte
posts.content: The second iteration of this library was released recently: it is superior to this library in every way except raw speed. Check it out! Neural Network Library 2.0 Community Resources A little under a year since my last release, it looks like it’s that time again. With more neural network knowledge and understanding of OOP, I was able to build a worthy successor to my first library, found here . Other than being made professionally rather than for practice, the new library boasts a high-quality documentation website, trial LSTM neural networks, as well as a Github repository. What is this library? For those unfamiliar with my old library or machine learning in general, it … After seeing a friend ( @Auxintic ) playing around with Roblox neural networks and learning how to make them, a sense of competition drove me to outdo him for absolutely no reason other than as an excuse to understand machine learning myself. For the last few weeks, I have researched neural networks from the ground up and have designed a neural network library whose sole purpose is to be as customizable and open source as possible. To my knowledge, this is the first Roblox module that encompasses the features below while being easy to use in any application and open source. Now, most of you are scratching your heads: What are they for, what are they? Neural Network Use Neural networks are usually used in places where a normal behaviour tree based AI is impractical or far too difficult to code. AI that adapts to the player mid-interaction, AI that predicts what a player will do, AI that finds hidden trends to identify something in a pile of data, self-driving cars, etc. Many of us have seen such examples, like a bot that learned how to play perfect chess/checkers, bots that use images of our brainwaves to picture what we are thinking about (real thing by the way!), and of course, self-driving cars. On Roblox, by far the most famous example is @ScriptOn ’s AI car project : Though the training will be tedious, this is all possible with neural networks. This library, for example, is capable of doing anything and everything ScriptOn used in his project, even the visualizer. But first, let’s answer the other question: what ARE neural networks? Artificial Neural Networks: Explained Neural networks are digital mechanisms that mimic how neurons work in our brains. The main and most common type of NNs (neural networks) are “feedthrough” networks that primarily work on the concept of “weights” and “biases”. But, what are those? artificial_neural_network_1-791x388 791×388 46.9 KB You can see that a “feedthrough” NN consists of an input layer, a multitude of hidden layers, and an output layer. Each layer has a varied number of nodes, our artificial little neurons. Each one of these nodes contains some data inside, namely the “weights” and “bias”. The “weights” are numbers that are multiplied against a previous node’s output to increase or decrease its importance and impact. A weight is assi

posts.author: TritiumAndDeuterium
posts.content: One question I do wanna ask, is… any reason you put (ANN) in your title? What does it stand for / mean?

posts.author: Kironte
posts.content: It stands for “Artificial Neural Network”. This is because there are many types of neural nets meant for different purposes. There are ANNs, CNNs (convolutional), and much more. This library, however, only focuses only on ANNs.

posts.author: TritiumAndDeuterium
posts.content: What is ANN’s purpose? Like could I make this neural network learn where a zombie / npc would get stuck in the map, and find out which direction to turn to get unstuck?

posts.author: Kironte
posts.content: In general, ANNs work for any problem that has a sane amount of inputs/outputs (for example, not images), and when it doesn’t need to be too complex. A better way to summarize it: ANNs are the first network you try. If for whatever reason it doesn’t fit, you proceed to another type of network. ANNs will most likely work well for anything on Roblox. Think of it like algebra; its the first thing you try. If it doesn’t work, you try Calculus and so on.

posts.author: TritiumAndDeuterium
posts.content: Kironte: sane I don’t want to be mean, but I don’t understand what sane means. Did you mean same?

posts.author: Kironte
posts.content: Sane meaning within reason. On Roblox, the max inputs/outputs you can really work with is about 500-700. Past that, you’re on your own. This is why ANNs don’t work well for images; you can’t have an input for every color of every pixel. Plus it would take centuries to train it properly. This is why Convolutional Neural Networks exist; for images recognition. However, their structure is very different and is a huge task on its own to create.

posts.author: RRandomkrys
posts.content: This is a wonderful module! Great work! I used to do neural networks back in the day (made a library from scratch in JavaScript, but mostly just stole back propagation math lol). However, a few questions about the genetic aglorithm: Have you considered adding a completely random network every generation? This may assist in nudging you out of local minimums (for others out there, a local minimum is when a network has perfected a task with the resources given, but still has room for improvement. Think of it like a minefield: you have to push it out of one deep hole to get it into the deepest one. Adding more randomness is one simple way to prevent this). Unfortunately though, near the end of training it may do more harm than good by eating resources. Why not clone the best network and tweak the clone? This will both preserve the original while still allowing the genetic algorithm to attempt to perfect it. Great work my friend, I’m glad you were able to pull this off! Personal question: Do you understand the math behind it fully? I myself watched a lot of Coding Train and took the back propagation algorithm from him (gotta love Dan). I’m considering trying to learn the math this semester in CS, but we’ll see. Thanks!

posts.author: TritiumAndDeuterium
posts.content: Kironte: module.backwardNet(network,0.01,{xCoo,yCoo},{correctAnswer}) In your example, what would 0.01 be?

posts.author: Kironte
posts.content: DatOneRandomDude: Have you considered adding a completely random network every generation? Yes, I have considered adding a completely random network every generation. However, I decided not to do this because it would do very poorly in small size generations and is basically a more extreme form of the mutations. If you want more randomization, you can just adjust the mutation parameters manually (will probably add an option for that later). DatOneRandomDude: Why not clone the best network and tweak the clone? Though I do save the best network as is for the next generation, any more attention to it would be bad for the network. If you stick too much to 1 network, you will hit a local minimum and won’t be able to get out of it. This is why instead of having the best network as one of the parents for every child, I do it randomly. This adds the measure of variance that makes genetic algorithms useful. DatOneRandomDude: Do you understand the math behind it fully? Though the SGD algorithm is rather tricky, watching enough 3brown1blue videos alongside some math explanations allowed me to understand the math behind it. You won’t be able to get all the information compatible with your brain from 1 single video; you have to watch a whole series of them until you start to understand this complex concept. Thanks for the support, hope you enjoy it!

posts.author: Kironte
posts.content: DragRacer31: In your example, what would 0.01 be? 0.01 would be the learning rate. 0.01 is a safe bet because though it does make progress very slow, when working with math function problems, precision is extremely important. There is a low chance you’ll hit a point from which you can’t progress (local minimum, @DatOneRandomDude explains it well above). However, you can easily increase it to 0.1 and get more of less the same results.

posts.author: TritiumAndDeuterium
posts.content: Kironte: 0.01 would be the learning rate So does that mean the higher that this is, then the faster it will learn?

posts.author: Kironte
posts.content: DragRacer31: So does that mean the higher that this is, then the faster it will learn? Yes but this comes at the cost of lower precision; by setting a higher rate, you’re limiting how precise the network can get. This is why there are optimizer algorithms other than SGD that fix this problem, like “Momentum”.

items.title: Improving user experience by reducing exposure of low quality content

posts.author: starrrydays
posts.content: Hi Creators, Today, we’d like to share an update on how we are improving user experience by reducing exposure of low quality content. Building a good experience on Roblox is a labor of love, and we want to make sure experiences that offer unique value to the community don’t have their discoverability impacted by low quality content. Three areas we are improving upon today Ensuring your creative work shines: Experiences with thumbnails, title, description or placefiles that closely resemble existing experiences on Roblox are no longer prioritized for recommendations (across Home, Charts) and are now less visible in search results. This doesn’t mean creators can’t make experiences in the same trend as existing content; they can, as long as they add differentiated value with unique content and accurately represent their uniqueness in the title, images, description, and placefiles. This ensures users find the unique and diverse content they are looking for. Safeguarding users from implied Robux giveaways: Experiences that imply any type of monetary rewards, such as free Robux are now less visible. Building user trust in metadata: When the metadata and content are highly mismatched, that content will not be recommended to users across Home and Charts. It will also be less visible in search. The title, thumbnail, genre, description, and age guidelines need to accurately reflect the actual experience. This will help users make informed decisions about the content they interact with. How to increase your reach Discoverability is within your reach if you build a great experience that users enjoy and put these best practices into action. Be accurate. Avoid using irrelevant keywords to ensure your content is discoverable. Follow our metadata best practices . Publish content with unique metadata. Focus on using original imagery and naming that you or your teammates created to help your experience stand out. Avoid publishing content with repetitive titles and images that have been previously published by another creator. To ensure your thumbnails are effective, test them with thumbnail personalization . Stick to the same network of groups and accounts when publishing sequels and spin-offs to your existing experience. This way, our systems know that it is connected to you, and your sequel/spin-off can continue to enjoy discoverability. Add your own spin to existing trends in the title, images, description, and in-experience content when you hop on trends. Your unique updates let us know that you’ve added value that is differentiated from other experiences following that trend. Experiences that don’t follow the above best practices will not rank high in search and will not be eligible for homepage recommendations. You will see a message on your creator dashboard if we detect your experience has a quality issue, and you will find detailed best practices in Creator Hub to improve your reach. 1447×314 47.5 KB Discoverability is dynamic, and our systems reclassify
