posts.author: TechSpectrum
posts.content: Web Frameworks and Libraries Table of Contents Read Chapter About There are a couple of popular web frameworks and libraries out there that are very common and we’re going to talk about a few of them. Currently is this tutorial you’ve seen we have used React. In this chapter we’ll go into detail about the role React would survive in a development and about who created it. Learning this technologies is important but you should preferably focus on mastering one of them based on whatever is popular in your area in relation to job openings. Bootstrap This is a popular library that makes cross-platform compatibility much easier to accomplish. In today’s world we’re often using our phones to browse more than with our desktops. Back then it was uncommon and technology and design philosophy wasn’t really well developed yet as we tried to make the website be responsive to the size of the screen. This is regularly used next to web frameworks that play on some of Bootstraps pre-built components. Angular This is a framework created by a small Google Team and is actively maintained by them. Now Angular has gone through a scizm during it’s development. AngularJS as it’s now known is the original version of Angular released in 2010 that relied on Javascript and jQuery. In 2016 Angular 2 was released, it was a version of the framework so different and rewritten it was incompatible with the old AngularJS structure which caused some outrage. Angular 2 (Now called just ‘Angular’) uses TypeScript which is a modified version of Javascript that uses Object Orientated Programming (OOP) syntax and logic. ironically, Google does not actually use their own framework for most if any of the websites however it is a full fledged tool that is still strong and being used. It’s most popular for Single Page Application (SPAs) websites that don’t involve loading new pages but updating a single page. Some website using AngularJS and Angular (2+). React Unlike Angular, React is actually used by it’s own creators – facebook. On it’s own React is just a library with routing capabilities for pages. React also has a very active community that has developed extensions that can turn React into a complete framework when combined with add ons and tools required to develop a website. They also take a component based approach, where each section of a website such as a footer, sidebar, and header are all separate objects that can be reused on a web page where as Angular relies on update a single page without the need to re-load assets. It’s very popular for multi-page websites. Websites using React Security and Attacks Table of Contents Read Chapter DoS and DDoS Table of Contents Denial of Service (DoS) is an attack aimed to make the service unavailable to other users who need it. Methods of Denial Redirect - This can be done by redirecting traffic to another site, usually by cross site scripting which we’ll talk about soon. Close Connections - Making the host ports unaccessible that the serv

posts.author: TechSpectrum
posts.content: Roblox Scripting Table of Contents Read Chapter HttpService Table of Contents API Reference What this service allows is for us to make http request to sites outside of Roblox, but you are not able to send request to Roblox. This would mean you would need a proxy server which is basically a server that you send a request to so it can do the intended request for you and send back the same information for you to use. It’s important to know that Roblox has their own rate limit on it’s usage where you can only send 500 request per minute before it stalls for 30 seconds. Before you can use the service remember to turn it on in studio under game settings image 1660×556 45.6 KB The service has three kinds of functions we can use, GetAsync , PostAsync , and RequestAsync which is new and has no restrictions on the request type such as allowing for multiple methods (i.e. Put, Delete, etc.). We’ll be using the new RequestAsync function for flexibility. Making a Request Let’s look over some example code provided by the wiki first. local HttpService = game:GetService("HttpService") local response = HttpService:RequestAsync{ Url = "http://httpbin.org/post"; Method = "POST"; Headers = { ["Content-Type"] = "application/json"; }; Body = HttpService:JSONEncode{hello = "world"}; } if response.Success then print("Status code: " .. response.StatusCode .. " " .. response.StatusMessage) print("Response body:\n" .. response.Body) else print("The request failed: " .. response.StatusCode .. " " .. response.StatusMessage) end So obviously, we’re going to get the service first before we can use it. Now we just have to break down the function and what it is we’re doing with it. Url - Your website link, now assuming you setup your own server using Heroku or Digital Ocean you could use your own web server based on the domain you purchased the records you setup. If setup properly you can provide it. Method - The type of request that’s being made, and you can have multiple on the same web route. The most regular methods you’ll find are GET , POST and less commonly PUT and DELETE . Header - With roblox you typically want to send JSON as it’s a standard in most cases. However you could also provide a few more details such as if the website the request is being made for may want security details in the header. Body - This is variables you’ll find, in the example case when we basically saying: http://httpbin.org/post?hello="world" in JSON form which our Express App we convert into a Javascript Object to be used. If we were to use our own server and based on our REST API chapter it would be a request that looks like this: local userId = 12030293 local response = HttpService:RequestAsync{ Url = "https://mywebsite.com/api/users/"..userId; Method = "GET"; Headers = { ["Content-Type"] = "application/json"; }; } Keep in mind how you handle request and don’t hit the limit. Discord Table of Contents Read Chapter This chapter is a rehash of the original thread! Creating a Bot First we visit t

posts.author: TechSpectrum
posts.content: Docker Table of Contents Read Chapter Docker is a system that allows for you to take an application and store it in a container. It acts as a virtual OS environment on the application level inside an existing machine while not having the same overhead and abundance of needless resources if you were to run the application on multiple servers. Its a great way to contain your project, if you spread your application into microservices. A microservice is a subset of parts such as: a web page, authentication server, database, and API. Instead of running each as a single application you are dockerizing the parts so if one fails the rest can still run just fine. When you combine technology with Kubernetes you can scale your microservices to fit the demand of the traffic or replace a dead docker that crashed and stopped responding. Kubernetes Table of Contents Read Chapter Kubernetes was developed by Google and open sourced for development such as with the company Red Hat. This technology was created to vastly improve web scaling where we use to only rely on techniques such as spreading traffic to other servers running duplicates of usually the exact project. What kubernetes does is allow you to run your dockerized services in clusters, what this means is you can take the web page service and if it gets too much traffic the kubernetes will orchestrate your environment so that it creates another docker to send traffic to reducing the stress on one application. This is extremely useful because it scales what needs to be increased and reduces where work isn’t needing essentially optimizing your servers full potential. If you ever want to work on a web service that you expect to have a large amount of traffic or even just want to optimize and make your service perform to the best of its ability for the future by having the work done early, I suggest you look into using kubernetes and its widely growing and now supported on Digital Ocean. Career Education Table of Contents Read Chapter Job Types Frontend HTML, CSS, Javascript Web Designer User Experience (UX) Designer Backend Database Developer Javascript, PHP, SQL Data Analyst Statistics Database Languages (SQL) Job Searching For those of you who want to pursue a career in the field of web development you have to understand a few things such as the resources available to you, and how to find what you want to do. To start lets think about what your area you live in is, you want to understand what’s popular around you because some business may be in demand for database analyst, angular, or react web designers. It’s important to look into this by using a site to find local jobs: Job Sites: Monster Jobs Search - Find Jobs Near Me | Monster.com https://www.indeed.com/ https://www.glassdoor.com/index.htm https://www.ziprecruiter.com/ https://www.upwork.com/ (Freelance) Be sure to read what jobs are demanding carefully, you should also go to their website and see if you can apply directly through it. Also I wasn’t sure if I should put this in Tutorials or Resources so please tell me if I put it in the wrong category. I am going to be doing two parts to this because there would be too many characters if I only have one part. Crazedbrick1 Crazedbrick1 is a crazy talented builder within the Roblox community. Crazed has been developing on the platform since 2020 but started taking it seriously in 2021 when he started taking commissions. In that time he has contributed to more than 18M+ game visits. Crazed is mostly known for his stunning map environmental design, low poly Blender modeling, and so much more. Interview Highlights What advice would you give to people wanting to create a successful game? In my perspective, success is more than the number of people playing and the amount of income the game generates. A successful game is also something previously unheard of and starts trends, not joins them. Avoid making games based on fads in the community. Instead, make unique games that start a new era of games on the platform. Any advice you would share with developers in general? My biggest advice for all developers is to simply love what you do, and the money will follow. Do not develop on this platform if you solely want money. You will not be successful with that mindset. items.title: Make Player/GetNetworkPing accessible on the Client

posts.author: RuizuKun_Dev
posts.content: Currently it is too difficult to display the current player’s ping accurately without any delays or causing network strains. I am aware of methods such as using a ValueBase or Attributes however I do not believe that it is best practice to replicate this property every Replication step. nor should I be using RemoteEvents and RemoteFunctions which has a delay and will display inaccurate and outdated data to the players. therefore it is best for Roblox to make Player/GetNetworkPing accessible to LocalScripts, adding this will improve many developer’s experience as well as players. items.title: [Studio Beta] Introducing Procedural Models: Build Parametrized 3D Models with Code or AI

posts.author: tnavarts
posts.content: [Update] April 28, 2026 tnavarts: Hi Creators, We’ve been blown away by what you’re creating with procedural models. Thank you for all of your support! We wanted to provide a quick clarification on procedural models: When using Assistant or Studio MCP, you can include a reference image with your prompt. This provides extra context and helps ensure that the generated model more accurately reflects what you want. Key Takeaways Procedural Models now let you build parametric 3D objects using code or AI. This automates repetitive building tasks and allows for instant, non-destructive resizing. This is available in Roblox Studio to all creators under the “Edit-Time Procedural Models” Studio beta today. Hi Creators, Today we’re launching Procedural Models , built on top of a new Instance type that lets you quickly create 3D models that automatically adapt to customizable attributes you define. This lets you dynamically adjust a model’s geometry, colors, or materials just by changing the value of different attributes. This is available in Roblox Studio to all creators under the “Edit-Time Procedural Models” Studio beta today! Enabling the “Edit-Time Procedural Models” Beta Feature: Open the Beta Features panel in the system menu under “File → Beta Features”. Check “Edit-Time Procedural Models” enabling it. Restart Studio. To get started, go to Studio, Insert Object, and select ProceduralModel . It comes with a ready-to-use template. What are Procedural Models? A ProceduralModel is a smart container. When you change the Size property or an attribute (like “Spacing” or “Material”), it triggers a Generator Module—a Luau script that rebuilds the model for you. Build Faster: Create buildings, bridges, or foliage that can regenerate based on parameters you set. Creator-defined Customization: Use custom attributes to drive design changes, like stair height or shelf count, and see results immediately. Engine-Level Integration: By filling in a single OnGenerate function, you get automatic integration with the Studio undo stack, Team Create, Dragger tools, and the ability to use Procedural Models both while editing and (once out of beta) at runtime. Idle until Activated: Procedural Models behave like standard models until a parameter changes. They don’t come with any extra execution overhead. Creating Generator Modules To get started, use Insert Object in Studio to insert a ProceduralModel . The inserted ProceduralModel will come with a helpful template to work from. A ProceduralModel gets its parameters from the ModuleScript that its Generator property points to. This could either be a ModuleScript located under the ProceduralModel itself or in a common shared location like ReplicatedStorage . The Generator should define two things: An Attributes map specifying which attributes trigger re-generation of the model and the default values of these attributes. The default values will be automatically added to the ProceduralModel if not already there. An OnGenerate fun

items.title: [Beta] Introducing Automated RTBF Processing for Data Stores

posts.author: LLOR3NZ
posts.content: Key Takeaways We’re introducing the beta for Automated Right to be Forgotten Processing (RTBF) for Data Stores. Every time a RTBF request hits your inbox, you can now define deletion “templates” once for your data and use Automated RTBF Processing to action against your Data Stores. Read the post to see if your experience is eligible. [Update] April 24, 2026 LLOR3NZ: Hi everyone, We’re happy to share that UI support for Automated RTBF Processing is now live! You can now view, create, and edit your RTBF templates directly from the Creator Hub. This provides a much more streamlined way to manage your data compliance without having to manually interface with the DataStoresConfig repository via API. Where to find it: Head to the Creator Hub. Select your Experience. Navigate to Data Stores Manager and select the RTBF Deletion tab. We’ve updated our Onboarding Guide to include the UI workflow. As always, please let us know if you have any feedback or encounter any issues! Hi Creators, Managing Right to be Forgotten (RTBF) requests should take minimal time away from building your experience. To help you streamline your workflow and reduce your manual effort to process user deletion requests, we’re excited to launch the Beta of Automated RTBF Processing for Data Stores. Instead of spending engineering overhead to set up a custom RTBF webhook, maintaining your own automation, or hunting down data every time a RTBF request hits your inbox, you can now define deletion “templates” once for your data and use Automated RTBF Processing to action against your Data Stores. How It Works Automated RTBF uses Templates to identify data belonging to a specific user. By providing a pattern that includes a {UserId} token (e.g., PlayerData_{UserId} ), you tell the RTBF Processor to automatically delete the corresponding entries associated with a user submitting a RTBF request. For Standard Data Stores , the deletion that the Automated RTBF Processor performs on the data store or key is a “soft delete”, similar to that of calling the Open Cloud Deletion APIs or RemoveAsync() in a game server. Therefore, the data is recoverable within 30 days should you have a reason to. To restore a deleted data store, use the “Restore” button on Data Stores Manager , or use the Undelete Data Store Open Cloud Endpoint . To restore a deleted key, see the documentation on Data store versioning . For Ordered Data Store keys, the data is deleted immediately and is unrecoverable. We recommend testing out the RTBF flow on a test environment / experience first to confirm your RTBF templates are configured properly. Onboarding Guide This feature stores your RTBF templates within the Experience Configs system. This is a centralized system for managing experience-wide settings. For the beta, you will be able to submit your templates to the DataStoresConfig repository using the Configs API . See the Automated RTBF onboarding guide here for detailed instructions on how to use this new feature. Is You

items.title: [Full Release] Introducing CreateDataModelContent: Convert editable mesh and image data into static content

posts.author: FGmm_r2
posts.content: Key Takeaways AssetService:CreateDataModelContent is now available as a Studio beta. This allows you to convert editable meshes and images into static assets that exist and replicate within a Data Model session. This allows you to create and use procedurally created content in more flexible ways. Find this API under the AssetService in the latest Studio build. [Update] May 18, 2026 FGmm_r2: We are excited to announce that CreateDataModelContent is now available in published experiences. With this API you can now “bake” editable mesh and image data into static Content for the lifetime of the session when you no longer need to edit them. Please continue providing bug reports, feedback and sharing what you have made in this dev forum post. Hi Creators, We’re excited to introduce AssetService:CreateDataModelContentAsync , a new API that allows you to “bake” editable mesh and image data into static Content for the lifetime of the session when you no longer need to edit them. This static content works just like any URI-backed mesh or image asset with an asset ID ( rbxassetid:// Asset ID ): They can be used to drive MeshContent and TextureContent properties on instances like MeshPart. They can replicate between the server and clients when needed. They are transcoded into various Levels of Detail (Meshes) and Mips (Images) for optimized client-side delivery. They do not use up Editable* memory budgets. They can be converted back into EditableMesh and EditableImage Objects for editing. They also have some key differences: They are scoped to the lifetime of the current data model session unlike URI-backed assets, which persist forever. They do not need to be uploaded / published as assets in your inventory. They do not need to go through moderation first (see Trust and Safety section for more information) You directly reference them as Content in your Luau scripts instead of using Asset IDs. If created on the server, they replicate down to clients with optimized distribution just like instance or property replication while uri-based assets are delivered directly from the cloud. These similarities and differences make them a very powerful tool for in-experience creation when coupled with EditableMesh and EditableImage APIs. Hundreds of unique, procedurally generated, in-experience donuts created using EditableMesh + EditableImage and then “baked” (pun intended) into data model content. Getting Started Enable the Beta: In Studio, go to File Beta Features and check the box for EditableMesh/Image to static content conversion API Restart Studio: You must restart for the new features to become available. You will now have access to AssetService:CreateDataModelContentAsync in your scripts. Working with Data Model Content Creating with AssetService:CreateDataModelContentAsync() “Baking” an editable Object into Data Model Content requires a single async API call. The following code snippet shows how you can use it to bake an EditableMesh and EditableImage and then

items.title: [Full Release] GetFriendsWhoPlayed API: Build Scalable Friend Leaderboards and Social Engagement Loops

posts.author: PMRoblox_1190
posts.content: Key Takeaways With the new GetFriendsWhoPlayed DevAPI, you can quickly build in-game friends leaderboards and other social features by only querying friends who’ve played your game, dramatically reducing DataStore reads and complexity so you can focus on developing gameplay. Use the API in Studio today. [Update] June 25, 2026 PMRoblox_1190: The GetFriendsWhoPlayed DevAPI is now fully released for all games to use. Try it out now in Studio, and let us know your feedback and what features you’d like to see in the future. Hi Creators, Friend leaderboards are one of your most requested social features, but today they’re expensive to run at scale because you have to fan out DataStore reads across a user’s entire friend list. We’re releasing the beta for our new GetFriendsWhoPlayed API that gives you a way to fetch only friends who have actually played your game. Now, you can build fast, reliable friends leaderboards with far fewer reads. We will expand access over time as we validate performance and reliability. What is the GetFriendsWhoPlayed API? The GetFriendsWhoPlayed API lets you query friends who have played this universe ever. Instead of pulling a full friend list and hitting DataStore for everyone, you can now narrow down to the friends who matter and then look up scores only for them. This reduces DataStore load, lowers the chance of rate limits, and makes it more practical to run friends leaderboards at scale. Implementing a Friends Leaderboard The beta introduces a new method on Player: local DataStoreService = game:GetService("DataStoreService") local GlobalScores = DataStoreService:GetOrderedDataStore("GlobalScores") local function getFriendLeaderboard(player) local friendScores = {} -- 1. Fetch friends who played recently local friendIds = player:GetFriendsWhoPlayedAsync() -- 2. Fetch score of each of the friends who played for _, friendId in friendIds do local success, score = pcall(function() return GlobalScores:GetAsync(tostring(friendId)) end) if success and score then table.insert(friendScores, { UserId = friendId, Score = score, }) end end return friendScores end API shape (beta) Player:GetFriendsWhoPlayedAsync() → Array of userIds (Type: Number) Returns a list of friends’ user IDs who have played the same games. See documentation here . Other Use Cases Beyond Leaderboards Although the main goal is powering friends leaderboards, this API is meant to be broadly useful for any feature that needs “friends who actually play this game,” such as: “Friends who play this game” sections in menus Social surfaces that highlight active friends What’s Next In the future, we’re planning to provide out-of-the-box support for friend and global leaderboards on Roblox platform surfaces, designed to drive stronger competition and deeper player engagement. Please share any feedback with us below. Thank you! FAQs Does this replace OrderedDataStore or my existing leaderboard system? No. This API does not store scores or rank players. It helps you figure

items.title: Update on Safety & Privacy: Introducing Scoped User Identifiers

posts.author: buffyreal123
posts.content: Key Takeaways Scoped User IDs are now available for early testing. This update assigns a unique user ID to players for each game, preventing cross-game tracking and protecting user privacy. You may opt in to provide feedback, otherwise no action is required. [Update] June 10, 2026 LeafGreen888: Hello Creators, Looking through the feedback, I noticed a few common questions and concerns popping up. Let me try to clear those up: For the large vast majority of games - this will have minimal impact, and things will largely be invisible behind the scenes. You do not have to do any code updates, you do not have to do any DataStores migrations, and you can continue to use DataStores as you have today. Your players do not have to do any changes either. Your game may continue to interface with players through user ID including looking up players by user ID in DataStores Manager or your in-house tooling. For groups of games that have cross-game player journeys , such as progression sharing or gift sharing - this will impact those journeys. However, we will be providing an account linking solution where players may provide user permission consent, which will then allow your games to look-up a consented player’s user ID of another game. We will provide this functionality before the broader rollout of Scoped User IDs in October. For groups of games that have shared moderation (i.e., ban lists) - this will be impacted. However, we will be providing Ban API improvements to facilitate cross-game sharing of ban lists, and we will ensure this solution is available before the broader rollout of Scoped User IDS in October . At this time, we invite you to share with us your use cases and feature requests. We will continue to review comments for more nuanced topics. Hi Creators, We’re launching an early preview and rollout timeline for Scoped User IDs , which will protect user privacy across games. Our goal is for players to safely enjoy Roblox with greater data privacy controls. Why Scoped User IDs Today, every player has one Global User ID used everywhere: in your games, in APIs, and on Roblox’s website. This means it can be used to follow players across games, and users have less control over how their information is used. To keep Roblox safe for users of all ages, we’re introducing Scoped User IDs. Players will have a unique User ID for each game that they visit. This mitigates the ability to associate users with a single Global User ID, and enables better protection of user privacy. This change will only take effect for new users of a game: anyone whose first visit happens after we roll out Scoped User IDs. Returning users to a game they’ve already played will continue to be referenced by their Global User ID. As more players try new games over time, Scoped User IDs will become the majority of references. Try Out Scoped User IDs The Player.User property is now available in Studio and creator-facing Engine APIs. Engine APIs that accept a User ID, such as Marketplac

posts.author: TheNexusAvenger
posts.content: I get the desire for the change, but this is extremely destructive for moderation tooling to the point where we can’t moderate our communities anymore. Assuming usernames will also be able to be withheld at some point ( my guess is they will ), users will be able to commit serious offenses without any way of us identifying them. For automated flagging or ban systems, it will also mean we have no clue who we are banning. Just that we are banning “somebody”. Appeal systems also become harder because now we need to reverse the global user id to a scoped one to get player data, which it doesn’t seem like an Open Cloud API will exist for yet. We also won’t be able to ban users within a community . Someone who does something to deserve a “Google Dockey!” in Innovation Inc Thermal Power Plant can’t then be banned from the Innovation Security Training Facility, or even Innovation Security. The linking API is what we need to solve it, but the users we need it for are the one who will say no to the prompts. And no, I do not trust “Report Abuse” to do it’s job.

items.title: My farewell - Mass open-sourcing of my work

posts.author: Tomi1231
posts.content: Since Roblox is effectively a dumbster fire that has since been buried 12 (not 6!) foot underground, here is my farewell. Use this as you wish. Most of these are bound to Roblox, but others can be used with luau runtimes, or used for learning Over the years, I have published many resources on this forum, which you can find by checking my profile. I will not be updating them anymore, so feel free to adopt any of my resources (and if you do, please let me know, so I can link to your post). One exception is LegacyChat ported to TextChatService , which I still have an update planned for, but tbh, it may take multiple months, or never happen Do not expect documentation, and do not be a cry baby about it. I will still answer questions, if I am still on the forum (I’ll probably still check it for some time) - Games I have uncopylocked the following games BloxBox: (not recommended for learning purposes, as it was my first game) Hub (Starting Place) Template (Template Sub Place for the [Create/Save]PlaceAsync api UpdateWorld (Sub Place where I would develop the next updates) BloxBox has a very advanced, and very good building system that you could strip out and reuse. The Resize code however is not the best, as I didn’t yet have linear algebra knowledge, and managed to achieve my goals with CFrame shenanigans instead… (aka linear algebra in disguise) Notes You must enable these settings (in the place settings of the template place) for the [Create/Save]PlaceAsync api to work image 508×318 22.4 KB Game Discovery Hub: (Fairly complex game discovery system. The UI code is horrendous though) Game Discovery Hub Proxy.zip (20.9 KB) Note that I will still be running GDH for the near future (running the proxy) Game Discovery Hub requires a self hosted proxy to work. I only run the proxy periodically, as the “game” can still work without the proxy, but charts wont get updated (and after ~ a week, the charts wont display any games). Since Roblox started allowing some of the roblox api endpoints from roblox servers, the proxy may or may not still be needed (although, I wanted to store the games database on the proxy instead of inside datastores, which would have advantages) Notes You have to configure two secrets, "API_SecretKey" (key that the proxy checks for authorization) and "API_Domain" , the url to the proxy ( subdomain.domain.com :port) Inside the proxy, in server.luau , you have to write down a key, set the port number, and then there is some freedns stuff, because that is what I was using, that you’d also have to set if you use it as well. In Utils/OpenCloud.luau , you’ll have to set the open cloud key, change the UniverseId from my game to your game in the open cloud url, and then, on the roblox creator website thing, setup open cloud to have read access to the Logs key in the ProxyLogs Datastore To run the proxy, you will need lune , latest version should work. (you could probably convert it to zune or lute without too many issues). Make sure your current

items.title: Weekly Recap: April 20 - 24, 2026

posts.author: Bluff_006
posts.content: Key Takeaways This week, Roblox launched Enhanced Protection with mandatory phishing-resistant 2FA for creators, announced new pricing and earning tools for Private Servers, and rolled out Procedural Models as a Studio beta for parametric 3D building with code or AI. Roblox Assistant also gained a Data Model Search subagent and Quick Connect for external MCP clients like Claude Code and Cursor, and Automated RTBF Processing for Data Stores now has full UI support in the Creator Hub. Check the full recap! Betas and Tests [Studio Beta] Introducing Procedural Models: Build Parametrized 3D Models with Code or AI Procedural Models let you build parametric 3D objects using code or AI, automating repetitive building tasks and enabling instant, non-destructive resizing. Available today to all creators in Roblox Studio under the “Edit-Time Procedural Models” Studio beta. [UPDATE] [Beta] Introducing Automated RTBF Processing for Data Stores UI support for Automated RTBF Processing is now live, so creators can manage RTBF templates directly in the Creator Hub via Data Stores Manager RTBF Deletion instead of manually interfacing with the DataStoresConfig repository via API. The onboarding guide has been updated with the new UI workflow. Updates and Announcements Assistant Updates: The Data Model Search Subagent and Quick Connect for MCP Clients The Data Model Search subagent lets agents investigate large workspaces in parallel without cluttering the main conversation. Quick Connect lets you connect external AI clients (Claude Code, Claude Desktop, Cursor, and others) to Studio with a single toggle instead of editing config files manually. Upcoming Updates to Private Servers: New Tools and New Earning Opportunities Starting April 30, creators can update prices every 60 days without canceling subscriptions, group revenue splits apply immediately to all future payments, and regional pricing is enabled automatically. Roblox Plus subscribers can access paid private servers at no cost and creators earn up to 100 Robux per subscriber per server, with the ability to sell private servers directly within games coming later. Introducing Enhanced Protection: Professional-Grade Security for Creators Enhanced Protection is now available, adding mandatory phishing-resistant 2FA (like passkeys and hardware keys) to secure assets and group payouts. Enable it through the Security tab in Account Settings. [UPDATE] Level Up Your Community: Assign Multiple Roles per Member Dual role auto-assignment on community join has changed: if you delete the lowest-ranked role in a community created before March 16, the system will no longer replace it with the next lowest one. The change was applied retroactively to communities where creators had already deleted roles after launch. Release Notes Release Notes for 717

items.title: New Advertising Policies & Standards

posts.author: superburr0
posts.content: Key Takeaways New Advertising Policies, Standards Tools will roll out starting in April. Our new Advertising Integrations policy will clarify the definition of ads on Roblox, going into effect on May 4. This will enable users to have a more consistent experience with advertising on the platform. All age segments on Roblox will now allow for age-appropriate ad categories and formats. All advertising formats on Roblox for U13 will be COPPA compliant and in line with industry best practices like other platforms (e.g., YouTube Kids). Rewarded Advertising formats will not be allowed for under 13 (U13). If you are running independent ads on Roblox, you will be required to register campaigns as Advertising Integrations in Ads Manager, and apply new standardized advertising labels in Studio, starting on May 4. All advertising integrations will have a revenue share between Roblox and the Creator, coming into effect in 2027. We’re finalizing the details in conversations with creators, and will share more in the coming months. [Update] June 17, 2026 superburr0: Hi Creators, Today, we’re sharing details on our new revenue share for Advertising Integrations, which goes into effect in early 2027. We’re also launching a new in-platform estimator in Ads Manager to provide you with certainty on your costs, and to help you set your pricing. This is another step that will establish a consistent, clear standard for pricing and reporting of Advertising Integrations on the platform, helping to grow long-term brand investment in the space and enable you to understand the value of your integrations. How the New Revenue Share Will Work For active integration campaigns, a cost will apply for every session with a qualified visit. A qualified visit will match what is reported to brands as an ad engagement, based on Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) elements like time-in-view, occlusion, and size. If a user engages with multiple branded assets in 1 game session , it only counts as 1 visit . This setup means there’s no penalty for big, immersive integrations that drive brand outcomes. In fact, it helps you set a higher value for deeper, higher quality engagements. Later this year, we’ll release reporting that you and brands will be able to access. This cost won’t apply to brands labeling their own assets as ads within their own games. It also excludes other deal elements, like influencers, that occur outside of the integration itself. How It’s Priced We list costs per 1,000 branded visits (or CPTV) to align with global advertising standards. Rates vary by geography to bring your integrations in line with international media prices. These costs aim to set a floor, so you’re still in full control of the price you set for your brand deals. We expect global CPTV averages at first to be around $4 - $5 as a guideline. Integrations that deliver high value in terms of time spent or effectiveness can charge more. This cost is active for the first 28 days of a campaign. After 28 day

posts.author: iphoneisstrong1234
posts.content: There is a problem with this policy. One important consideration: The Children and Teens’ Online Privacy Protection Act, aka CTOPPA or COPPA 2.0 (S.836) which has passed the Senate and is currently held at the desk in the House, would expand COPPA protections to all users under 17. This includes restrictions on targeted advertising, limits on data collection for ad delivery and measurement, and opt-in consent requirements for teens. Several parts of the new Advertising Integrations system – especially age-segmented ad delivery, rewarded formats for 13-17, and embedded brand content – may become difficult or impossible to operate under these rules if the bill becomes law. It may be worth evaluating how the upcoming policy aligns with these potential requirements. If these restrictions take effect, creators relying on Advertising Integrations for monetization may face sudden limitations or disruptions, especially for experiences with large teen audiences. Because the bill has already passed the Senate, it may be prudent to evaluate whether the current Advertising Integrations roadmap has a fallback plan for compliance if the restrictions take effect. Source: S.836 - 119th Congress (2025-2026): Children and Teens’ Online Privacy Protection Act | Congress.gov | Library of Congress

items.title: Weekly Recap: April 6 - 10, 2026

posts.author: Bluff_006
posts.content: Key Takeaway This week, the Character Controller Library hit full release, Roblox Plus was announced launching April 30, and Studio gained a Playtest Agent beta. We also saw Data Store storage limits go live and legacy game pass and developer product APIs set for deprecation on April 23. Check the full recap! Betas and Tests [Studio Beta] StyleQuery: More Styling Features StyleQuery is now in Studio beta. This feature lets you build responsive UI that adapts to layout constraints and global user settings without writing scripts. Enable it via the Beta Features tab in Studio. [Studio Beta] Studio Assistant MCP Playtest Agent Studio MCP and Assistant now include a dedicated playtest subagent that spawns a test character that agents can assign to perform basic playtesting in experiences. In Studio, go to File Beta Features and check Playtest Agent. [Beta] Introducing Automated RTBF Processing for Data Stores We’re introducing the beta for Automated Right to be Forgotten Processing (RTBF) for Data Stores. Define deletion “templates” once for your data and use Automated RTBF Processing to action against your Data Stores. Read the post to see if your experience is eligible. Updates and Announcements New Spatial Query Filters We’ve added a simpler and more flexible replacement for FilterDescendantsInstances to allow simultaneous exclude and include lists. [Full Release] The Future of Character Movement: Character Controller Library The Character Controller Library is now available for use in live experiences. Switching between the legacy Humanoid and the new Character Controller Library is available via Avatar Settings. Go to the Movement section of the Avatar Settings and select the Character Controller Library to try it out! Improved Audio Configuration for VideoFrame Instances VideoFrame now includes RollOffMaxDistance , RollOffMinDistance , and RollOffMode properties for precise control over how video audio fades over distance. VideoFrame.Volume will be capped at 10 on April 22 to match Sound instances. Find these properties in the Properties window when selecting a VideoFrame . Evolving Luau OSS: Community Contributions More We’re rolling out open-source community contributions and Wally/Creator Store publishing for our newest Luau repositories, including our new Signals library, and open-sourcing our CLI for running locally-developed Luau projects on Open Cloud. Introducing Roblox Plus Starting April 30, Roblox is introducing Roblox Plus, a new subscription designed for the most highly engaged users. As a creator, you can earn from Plus subscribers’ purchases, driving Plus sign-ups and subscribers spending time in your paid private servers. Testing More Recommended For You Algorithm Signals We’re testing broadening our algorithm signals to measure how players interact with your games across various timespans: from the first few minutes of a session, to daily, weekly and monthly. Once testing is done, the signals will be shared on the Creator analy

items.title: Allow the transfer of GamePasses between our own games

posts.author: Orange12345
posts.content: Roblox has recently announced that cross-game sales of GamePasses and DevProducts will be disabled on the 29th of May. This is a massive issue for certain people, myself included. Many years ago there was a limit on the amount of GamePasses you could create for a game, to get around this I created the passes on another game and simply allowed users to buy them through the in-game store. With this upcoming change I will have to recreate these passes on the main game. You can block players double buying via the in-game store. But there will be nothing stopping somebody from buying the recreated passes from the experience details page on the website. I ask that you allow developers to transfer GamePasses between their own games. This will stop a massive headache from the upcoming change. Even if you can’t add this as a feature to the website, it would be very helpful if you could transfer the GamePasses just this one time by asking a staff member to do it. Thank you.

posts.author: AmazingBarnabe
posts.content: Well… https://devforum.roblox.com/t/a-new-era-to-donations-cross-server-passes-linkd-are-you-interested/4622025?u=amazingbarnabe Linkd aside, you could still theoretically use the same system. Players can convert their Robux into a currency, eg, Gems. You can link that currency using Datastores in the cloud to other games. Finally, players can use that currency and top it up in any game, able to buy passes with that currency instead, rather than Robux. It’s a bit of a hassle, yes, but it’s the current alternative system that would work, and it essentially enables cross-server selling. (Not sure if OpenCloud has endpoints for accessing Datastores in another game though… but, you could always use a Datastore on a free website or something, which could work too.)

items.title: Weekly Recap: May 18 - 21, 2026

posts.author: frecklesnspectacles
posts.content: Key Takeaways Two major Full Release rollouts landed this week — Procedural Models for parametric 3D building and AssetService:CreateDataModelContentAsync for baking EditableMesh and EditableImage data into static content. For betas, Styling Transitions brings no-code UI tweens to Stylesheets, and the new GetFriendsWhoPlayed API powers efficient friend leaderboards. Monetization and policy updates also progressed, with higher Transfer limits now shipping for 2SV-secured accounts and Roblox Kids/Select publishing requirements beginning their regional rollout. Check the full recap! Betas and Tests [Beta] GetFriendsWhoPlayed API: Build Scalable Friend Leaderboards and Social Engagement Loops The new GetFriendsWhoPlayed DevAPI lets you quickly build in-game friends leaderboards and other social features by only querying friends who’ve played your game, dramatically reducing DataStore reads and complexity. Use Player:GetFriendsWhoPlayedAsync() in Studio today. [Studio Beta] Styling Transitions Styling Transitions enable no-code UI tweens within the Styling ecosystem, helping you uplevel your UI in a scalable, performant manner. Enable it in the Beta Features menu under Styling Transitions. Updates and Announcements [Full Release] Procedural Models: Build Parametrized 3D Models with Code or AI Procedural Models let you build parametric 3D objects using code or AI, automating repetitive building tasks and allowing for instant, non-destructive resizing. Try it now in Roblox Studio via Insert Object → ProceduralModel , with no opt-in required. More Control Over Importer: Custom Scale Factor and Updated Unit Conversions Customize how 3D content is scaled when importing with the new Scale Factor setting under File Geometry. Starting June 16, 2026, the conversion ratios for all non-Stud units will be updated to be consistent with a 25:7 Stud/meter ratio (previously 20:1). Boost your game earnings with Rewarded Video enhancements New Join with Reward placement on the experience details page and a no-code drag-and-drop Studio plugin make Rewarded Video easier to integrate and grow impressions. Simplified reporting now tracks Opt-In Rate, Frequency, fill rate, and reward rate, plus CXP testing for staged rollouts. [UPDATE] [Full Release] Introducing CreateDataModelContent: Convert editable mesh and image data into static content AssetService:CreateDataModelContentAsync is now available in published experiences, letting you bake EditableMesh and EditableImage data into static Content for the lifetime of the session. Baked content drives MeshContent and TextureContent , replicates server-to-client, and is freed from Editable* memory budgets. [UPDATE] Alternate Publishing Requirements for Roblox Kids and Select As of May 19, Roblox Kids and Select accounts began rolling out in New Zealand, the Netherlands, Australia, and Indonesia, with global launch in early June. The new Audience Reach tab on Creator Hub shows your game’s eligibility. [UPDATE] Update on Roblox P

posts.author: orangebird3alt
posts.content: Eight of these are great. The remaining one is okay but we need the 500 “highly-engaged” requirement for Kids and Select access to be adjusted for sure. Not sure how 'highly-engaged' players are determined. Definitely think this should be looked into. One of my games (which has around 40 CCU on average) has been stuck at 455/500 for the past few days, and because of this, my game is still stuck restricted to 16+ users only. Obviously, this number should move a LITTLE bit with two-digit CCUs, but no, just stuck at 455/500: image 2044×1278 140 KB We’ve satisfied all other requirements (and yes, the group holder account has Roblox Plus). Looking forward to seeing something done about this soon, we’re experiencing a huge loss of players after this update. items.title: New Publishing Requirements & Evaluation Process for Games

posts.author: skywise84
posts.content: Key Takeaways On May 19th, we’re making a change to our publishing model by adding new requirements to publicly publish games, helping keep Roblox online and users under 16 safer. You can check your eligibility in your account settings . [Update] May 11, 2026 skywise84: Hi Creators, As a reminder, on May 19, our new publishing requirements will begin rolling out. Also, we’re adding a one-time, refundable publishing fee of 1,000 Robux per game, as an alternative to having a Plus/Premium subscription to publish to all ages. Read the details here . [Update] April 17, 2026 We hosted a DevForum AMA on April 16 to share more about why we are making these changes. For your convenience, we’ve organized the answered questions by topics at the top of that post. Hi Creators, Every day, millions of users come to Roblox to play incredible games that all of you have built. Keeping the trust of users, their parents, and the policy makers who represent them is incredibly important, and helps make sure Roblox can continue to be accessed around the world. We know that the vast majority of you are dedicated to building high quality content that meets our Community Standards. Every day, we process billions of asset uploads submitted by millions of creators. While our existing moderation systems catch the vast majority of violations, no system is perfect and even a single infraction can cost us the trust of our community, which in turn can hurt well intentioned creators. Today we are announcing new publishing requirements and an evaluation process to help make sure games are suitable for our youngest users, which will go into effect on May 19th. Publishing games that are available to players with either Roblox Kids (users under 9) or Roblox Select (users 9 to 15) accounts that we announced in our Newsroom will require additional verification steps than publishing games that are available to users over 16.* While we know this is a lot of change and introduces requirements that creators didn’t have to meet before, it’ll allow us to set a new standard for the safety of games on Roblox and enable us to take stronger action on bad actors. They’ll also help us stay ahead of changing regulatory requirements, so your games can continue to be played by the millions of players that are on Roblox today. We want to answer the questions you have about these changes; we’ll be hosting an AMA on DevForum on April 16 at 12:30 pm to 2:30 pm PDT . We’ll share more about why we’re making these changes and take your questions live. * Exact ages of Roblox Kids and Roblox Select accounts may vary by region based on local requirements New Publishing Requirements You can check the status of your eligibilit y in your account settings to learn what actions you need to take. Publish for personal use - Anyone on Roblox can continue to publish games for personal use. Publish to 16+ and Trusted Friends - To publish a game that reaches Trusted Friends and users over 16 you must: a. Complete an age

items.title: [FREE] Advanced Backpack System🎒 [v1.2.1 Pre-1]

posts.author: rusted010101
posts.content: Hello! I’d like to introduce a new snapshot of my Backpack System, which is now in version v1.2.1 Pre-1 . This system is part of one of my projects called Project Asset , where I’ll be creating free-to-use assets and systems for developers. The current focus of Project Asset is to create a more advanced and expandable backpack system. v1.2.1 Pre-1 Changes (Shop system snapshot) This snapshot focuses on creating a reliable shop system, to be quite honest I expected this to be a quick update, taking 1-2 days to make a entire shop system, turns out my database was all messed up, I had to rewrite a bunch of it. Fixes Improvements Added ShopHandler module — registers shop zones by Part, runs a proximity detection loop using GetPartBoundsInBox to fire open/close events to clients as players enter and leave Added UserHandler module — full player profile management with .new() , .get() , :load() , :save() , :getData() and :setData() ; built on top of ProfileService Added ProfileService — session-locked DataStore backend for persistent player data Added DataTemplate — default player data structure (starts with Cash = 100 ) Added PlayersInShop shared state module — tracks which shop each player is currently in; used to block equipping and dropping items while inside a shop Added buy_event remote and server-side purchase handler — validates the request, checks and deducts leaderstats.Cash , transfers items to the player’s inventory, and deducts shop stock Added Inventory:Buy() method on the client — fires the buy request for a given slot and amount Context menu is now shop-aware: in a shop, the Drop button is relabelled to “Buy”, the amount field defaults to 1, and the equip button is disabled and labelled “Unused”; personal inventory behaviour is unchanged Dragging an item outside the inventory container while in a shop no longer triggers a drop Fixed price field fallback — item cost now reads cost first, then price , for compatibility Bug fix: personal inventory is correctly pushed back to the client when a player leaves a shop Player join now creates leaderstats with a Cash IntValue synced to the DataStore profile Player leaving now saves and releases the DataStore profile and cleans up shop state Added example FreeItemsShop model to the Workspace This project is extremely hard to upkeep with consistent updates, so there might be a slight increase in update/release/snapshot time, since all the basic features are already in the system, only thing’s missing are advanced systems, like the one we have here. Please do note that some comments may be unclear, documentation may be outdated, and the headers with version were not updated, since this update was mostly just foundations for future improvements. The Roblox place file containts a example script for onPlayerJoin , onToolEquipped and onDropped feel free to modify. Download Here’s the Roblox place download: BackpackSystem121_pre1.rbxl (184.2 KB) Showcase Showcase of the system above. image 1669×689 85.9 K

items.title: Leaderboards+ | The ultimate module for global leaderboards!

posts.author: p_droXD
posts.content: Leaderboards+ | The ultimate module for global leaderboards! Get in the Creator Store! Most leaderboard modules are built around one ranking style, usually just “highest value wins.” This one is a bit more flexible: you decide how entries should be compared, so it works for scoreboards, speedrun boards, lap times, or pretty much any other custom ranking. It also handles DataStore persistence, caching, and scheduled saves for you. Unlike traditional leaderboard systems that rely on OrderedDataStore , Leaderboards+ stores its data in a standard DataStore . This allows multiple leaderboard updates to be batched into a single UpdateAsync request when using scheduled saves, reducing DataStore API usage. Another advantage is that the entire leaderboard is available in memory after it’s loaded. Since OrderedDataStore only exposes entries through paginated results (up to 100 entries per page), determining the exact rank of an arbitrary player requires iterating through pages. With Leaderboards+ , every entry is already available in the cached leaderboard, making it trivial to determine any player’s rank. Features Storage Performance Uses a standard DataStore instead of OrderedDataStore Optional scheduled/batched saves to reduce DataStore API requests Automatically keeps the cached leaderboard synchronized after every UpdateAsync Queued saves automatically keep only each player’s best entry before writing Flexibility Fully customizable ordering function Supports any value type accepted by DataStoreService , not just numbers Default ordering is lowest value → highest value Optional player country storage Optional minimum and maximum numeric value filtering Leaderboard Utilities Instantly determine the rank of any player from the cached leaderboard Find players by UserId Clear the leaderboard during development or testing Events OnUpdate fires whenever the leaderboard changes OnNewWR fires whenever a new first-place entry is saved Example local Leaderboards = require("@game/ServerStorage/Leaderboards+") local Test = Leaderboards.new("Test") print(Test:Get()) Test.OnUpdate:Connect(function(Data) print("Leaderboard updated!", Data) end) Test.OnNewWR:Connect(function(UserId, Value, Country) print("New world record!", UserId, Value, Country) end) Test:SetSaveCountry(true) Test:SetMaxValue(100) Test:SetOrdering(function(A, B) return A.Value B.Value -- Highest value wins end) Test:Save(114213348, 88, true) -- Queued save Test:Save(389737388, 6) -- Immediate save API Creating a leaderboard local Board = Leaderboards.new("Coins") Returns an existing leaderboard if one with the same key has already been created. By default, leaderboards are sorted from lowest value to highest value . Saving Board:Save(UserId, Value, Schedule?) If Schedule is true , the entry is added to a save queue and written after the configured schedule time. Otherwise, it is written immediately. If multiple queued saves exist for the same player, the module automatically keeps only the player’s
